From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Japan's video game industry

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Daiichhisega.jpg/250px-Daiichhisega.jpg)](/wiki/File:Daiichhisega.jpg)

Sega [Akihabara](/wiki/Akihabara "Akihabara") Building 2, known as *GiGO* until 2017,[[1]](#cite_note-1) a former[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) large 6 floor [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega") game center on Chuo Dori, in front of the LAOX Aso-Bit-City in [Akihabara](/wiki/Akihabara "Akihabara"), [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), Japan, in 2006

[Video games](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") are a major industry in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), and the country is considered one of the most influential in video gaming.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Japanese game development is often identified with the [golden age of video games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games") and the country is home to many notable video game companies such as [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo"), [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega"), [Bandai Namco Entertainment](/wiki/Bandai_Namco_Entertainment "Bandai Namco Entertainment"), [Taito](/wiki/Taito "Taito"), [Konami](/wiki/Konami "Konami"), [Square Enix](/wiki/Square_Enix "Square Enix"), [Capcom](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom"), [NEC](/wiki/NEC "NEC"), [SNK](/wiki/SNK "SNK"), [Koei Tecmo](/wiki/Koei_Tecmo "Koei Tecmo"), [Atlus](/wiki/Atlus "Atlus"), [Arc System Works](/wiki/Arc_System_Works "Arc System Works"), [Marvelous](/wiki/Marvelous_(company) "Marvelous (company)"), [Nippon Ichi Software](/wiki/Nippon_Ichi_Software "Nippon Ichi Software"), [Level-5](/wiki/Level-5_(company) "Level-5 (company)"), [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") and formerly its branch [Sony Computer Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Interactive_Entertainment "Sony Interactive Entertainment"). In 2022, Japan was the [third largest video game market](/wiki/List_of_video_games_markets_by_country "List of video games markets by country") in the world after the [United States](/wiki/Video_games_in_the_United_States "Video games in the United States") and [China](/wiki/Video_games_in_China "Video games in China").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The space is known for the catalogs of several major publishers, all of whom have competed in the [video game console](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console") and [video arcade](/wiki/Video_arcade "Video arcade") markets at various points. Released in 1965, *[Periscope](/wiki/Periscope_(arcade_game) "Periscope (arcade game)")* was a major [arcade](/wiki/Video_arcade "Video arcade") hit in Japan, preceding several decades of success in the arcade industry there. Nintendo, a former [hanafuda](/wiki/Hanafuda "Hanafuda") playing card vendor, rose to prominence during the 1980s with the release of the home video game console called the [Family Computer](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System") (Famicom), which became a major hit as the [Nintendo Entertainment System](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System") (NES) internationally. Sony, already one of the world's largest electronics manufacturers, entered the market in 1994 with the [Sony PlayStation](/wiki/Sony_PlayStation "Sony PlayStation"), one of the first home consoles to feature [3D graphics](/wiki/3D_graphics "3D graphics"), almost immediately establishing itself as a major publisher in the space.[[6]](#cite_note-6) [Shigeru Miyamoto](/wiki/Shigeru_Miyamoto "Shigeru Miyamoto") remains internationally renowned as a "father of video gaming" and is the only game developer so far to receive Japan's highest civilian honor for artists, the 文化功労者 (bunka kōrōsha) or [Person of Cultural Merit](/wiki/Person_of_Cultural_Merit "Person of Cultural Merit").

Arcade culture is a major influence among young Japanese, with [Akihabara](/wiki/Akihabara "Akihabara") Electric Town being a major nexus of so-called [otaku](/wiki/Otaku "Otaku") culture in Japan, which overlaps with video gaming heavily. Japanese video game franchises such as *[Super Mario](/wiki/Super_Mario "Super Mario")*, *[Pokémon](/wiki/Pok%C3%A9mon "Pokémon")*, *[The Legend of Zelda](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda "The Legend of Zelda")*, *[Resident Evil](/wiki/Resident_Evil "Resident Evil"), [Silent Hill](/wiki/Silent_Hill "Silent Hill"), [Metal Gear](/wiki/Metal_Gear "Metal Gear"), [Devil May Cry](/wiki/Devil_May_Cry "Devil May Cry"), [Final Fantasy](/wiki/Final_Fantasy "Final Fantasy"),* *[Sonic the Hedgehog](/wiki/Sonic_the_Hedgehog "Sonic the Hedgehog"),* *[Fire Emblem](/wiki/Fire_Emblem "Fire Emblem")*, *[Super Smash Bros.](/wiki/Super_Smash_Bros. "Super Smash Bros.")*, *[Street Fighter](/wiki/Street_Fighter "Street Fighter")*, *[Kirby](/wiki/Kirby_(series) "Kirby (series)")*, *[Animal Crossing](/wiki/Animal_Crossing "Animal Crossing")*, *[Splatoon](/wiki/Splatoon "Splatoon")*, *[Xenoblade](/wiki/Xenoblade "Xenoblade")*, *[Dragon Quest](/wiki/Dragon_Quest "Dragon Quest")*, *[Tekken](/wiki/Tekken "Tekken")*, *[Kingdom Hearts](/wiki/Kingdom_Hearts "Kingdom Hearts"), [Persona](/wiki/Shin_Megami_Tensei:_Persona "Shin Megami Tensei: Persona")*, *[Dark Souls](/wiki/Dark_Souls "Dark Souls")*, *[Monster Hunter](/wiki/Monster_Hunter "Monster Hunter")* and many others have gained critical acclaim and continue to garner a large worldwide following. The [Japanese role-playing game](/wiki/JRPG "JRPG") is a major game genre innovated by Japan and remains popular both domestically and internationally, with titles like *Final Fantasy* and *Dragon Quest* selling millions. In 2018, the country had an estimate of 67.6 million players in its game market.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Gaming platforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Gaming platforms")]

### Arcade machines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Arcade machines")]

Further information: [Arcade video game](/wiki/Arcade_video_game "Arcade video game") and [List of Japanese arcade cabinets](/wiki/List_of_Japanese_arcade_cabinets "List of Japanese arcade cabinets")

### Consoles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Consoles")]

Further information: [Video game console](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console")

### PC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: PC")]

Further information: [Gaming computer](/wiki/Gaming_computer "Gaming computer")

### Mobile phones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Mobile phones")]

Further information: [Mobile game](/wiki/Mobile_game "Mobile game") and [Japanese mobile phone culture](/wiki/Japanese_mobile_phone_culture "Japanese mobile phone culture")

[Androids](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") and [iPhones](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone") reportedly had the highest player rate amongst its owners versus the player rate amongst owners of game consoles such as the [PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3 "PlayStation 3") and the [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii").[[8]](#cite_note-:1-8) This is most likely due to many Japanese people having daily, long commutes on the train or bus versus time at home and the mobile phone being portable and convenient.[[8]](#cite_note-:1-8) The main characteristic of mobiles games is being [freemium](/wiki/Freemium "Freemium"), and this monetization model is most commonly seen in [gacha games](/wiki/Gacha_game "Gacha game").[[8]](#cite_note-:1-8) In 2015, mobile game publishers had more than twice the ordinary income rate as console game publishers in Japan.[[8]](#cite_note-:1-8)

## Notable genres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Notable genres")]

### Action games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Action games")]

Main article: [Action game](/wiki/Action_game "Action game")

Action games cover a variety of subgenres including:

* [Battle royale games](/wiki/Battle_royale_game "Battle royale game")
* [Beat 'em up](/wiki/Beat_%27em_up "Beat 'em up")
* [Fighting games](/wiki/Fighting_games "Fighting games")
* [Hack and slash](/wiki/Hack_and_slash "Hack and slash")
* [Maze games](/wiki/Maze_games "Maze games")
* [Platformers](/wiki/Platformers "Platformers")
* [Rhythm games](/wiki/Rhythm_games "Rhythm games")
* [Shooter games](/wiki/Shooter_games "Shooter games")
* [Side-scrolling video games](/wiki/Side-scrolling_video_games "Side-scrolling video games")

### Gacha games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Gacha games")]

Main article: [Gacha game](/wiki/Gacha_game "Gacha game")

Gacha games are named after toy vending machines in Japan because of its lottery system.[[9]](#cite_note-:4-9) After spending in-game currency (which can be bought with real money), the player can get a random character or item.[[9]](#cite_note-:4-9) Most often, the characters that the player wants are rare and encourages the player to spend enough money until they get their desired character.[[9]](#cite_note-:4-9) Notable Japanese gacha games include *[iDOLM@STER](/wiki/The_Idolmaster_Cinderella_Girls "The Idolmaster Cinderella Girls")*, *[Granblue Fantasy](/wiki/Granblue_Fantasy "Granblue Fantasy")*, *[Fate/Grand Order](/wiki/Fate/Grand_Order "Fate/Grand Order")*, *[Umamusume: Pretty Derby](/wiki/Umamusume:_Pretty_Derby "Umamusume: Pretty Derby")* and others. Gacha games can often have different game elements such as combat and serve as an RPG or contain rhythm game game-play.

### Japanese role-playing games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Japanese role-playing games")]

Main article: [History of Eastern role-playing video games](/wiki/History_of_Eastern_role-playing_video_games "History of Eastern role-playing video games")

[Role-playing games (RPGs)](/wiki/Role-playing_game "Role-playing game") originated from [tabletop role-playing games (TRPGs)](/wiki/Tabletop_role-playing_game "Tabletop role-playing game") in the West such as [Dungeons & Dragons (D&D)](/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons "Dungeons & Dragons").[[10]](#cite_note-:2-10) In 1985, a Japanese-translated version of D&D was the first TRPG to be published in Japan.[[10]](#cite_note-:2-10) TRPGs were not as popular in Japan compared to overseas, but after [Enix](/wiki/Enix "Enix") published *[Dragon Quest](/wiki/Dragon_Quest "Dragon Quest")* in 1986, RPGs began to gain traction amongst Japanese consumers.[[10]](#cite_note-:2-10) Soon after, multiple Japanese publishers were releasing RPG franchises such as *[Final Fantasy](/wiki/Final_Fantasy "Final Fantasy")*, [*Persona*](/wiki/Persona_(series) "Persona (series)"), [*Tales*](/wiki/Tales_(video_game_series) "Tales (video game series)"), [*Pokémon*](/wiki/Pok%C3%A9mon_(video_game_series) "Pokémon (video game series)"), *[The Legend of Zelda](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda "The Legend of Zelda")*, [*Ys*](/wiki/Ys_(series) "Ys (series)"), *[Kingdom Hearts](/wiki/Kingdom_Hearts "Kingdom Hearts")*, and many others that were very popular with Japanese and overseas consumers.

The term "JRPG" was coined by Western media because Japanese-developed RPGs had distinct elements that set it apart from RPGs developed in the West.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The settings in Western RPGs tend to be inspired by fantasy and science-fiction literature while JRPGs have been inspired by [anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime") and [manga literature](/wiki/Manga "Manga").[[12]](#cite_note-:3-12) In addition, Western RPGs tend to be [open-world](/wiki/Open_world "Open world") and feature real-time combat systems, but JRPGs favor confining the player to smaller areas in the game world following a linear story line and most notably using turn-based combat systems like in D&D. Although there was typically less freedom in JRPGs compared to Western RPGs, the linear story lines allowed for Japanese developers to completely flesh out the character development of the player's companions and sometimes the player's character themselves.[[12]](#cite_note-:3-12) This focus on narrative was different from the West's focus on expression through RPGs and JRPGs appealed to players all over the world because of their rich story lines.[[12]](#cite_note-:3-12) However, as of recently, some Japanese developers are starting to try to appeal to a broader audience by introducing real-time combat, for example, such as *[Final Fantasy XVI](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_XVI "Final Fantasy XVI")*.[[12]](#cite_note-:3-12)

### Visual novels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Visual novels")]

Main article: [Visual novel](/wiki/Visual_novel "Visual novel")

[Visual novels (VNs)](/wiki/Visual_novel "Visual novel") are text-based story video games where players can usually make decisions at multiple points of the story and achieve different endings.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13) VNs are novel-like, but also include character visuals and artwork to complement with different scenes in the story and sometimes [voice acting](/wiki/Voice_acting "Voice acting").[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13) Many visual don't feature many game-play features besides decision-making. This decision-making differentiated visual novels from regular novels as it allowed players to replay the game at certain decision points to make different decisions and see different endings.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13) Most VNs are romance stories and allow the player to choose between multiple love interests and fall under [dating sims](/wiki/Dating_sim "Dating sim").[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13) VNs often contained sexual content in the beginning when VNs were first developed for the PC, but when VNs were starting to be published for game consoles as well, family-friendly versions were also released.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13) Like manga, popular VNs were also adapted into anime such as *[Higurashi: When They Cry](/wiki/Higurashi_When_They_Cry "Higurashi When They Cry")*, *[Clannad](/wiki/Clannad_(video_game) "Clannad (video game)")*, *[Fate/stay night](/wiki/Fate/stay_night "Fate/stay night")*, *[Steins;Gate](/wiki/Steins;Gate "Steins;Gate")*, and many others.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13)

Two big subgenres of VNs are [bishōjo](/wiki/Bish%C5%8Djo_game "Bishōjo game") and [otome](/wiki/Otome_game "Otome game") games. Bishōjo (meaning "beautiful girl" in Japanese) games are romance visual novels featuring a male protagonist with female love interests and are targeted towards straight men, while otome (meaning "maiden" in Japanese) games feature a female protagonist with male love interests catering towards straight women.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of video games](/wiki/History_of_video_games "History of video games"), [Arcade game](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game"), and [History of Eastern role-playing video games](/wiki/History_of_Eastern_role-playing_video_games "History of Eastern role-playing video games")

### Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Background")]

In 1966, [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega") introduced an [electro-mechanical game](/wiki/Electronic_game "Electronic game") called *[Periscope](/wiki/Periscope_(arcade_game) "Periscope (arcade game)")*[[14]](#cite_note-Kent-14) - a [submarine simulator](/wiki/Submarine_simulator "Submarine simulator")[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAshcraft2008133-15) which used lights and plastic waves to simulate sinking ships from a submarine.[[16]](#cite_note-16) It became an instant success in Japan, Europe, and North America,[[17]](#cite_note-Wolf-149-17) where it was the first arcade game to cost a [quarter](/wiki/Quarter_(United_States_coin) "Quarter (United States coin)") per play,[[14]](#cite_note-Kent-14) which would remain the standard price for arcade games for many years to come.[[17]](#cite_note-Wolf-149-17)

Sega later produced gun games that used [rear](/wiki/Rear_projection_effect "Rear projection effect") [image projection](/wiki/Image_projector "Image projector") in a manner similar to the ancient [zoetrope](/wiki/Zoetrope "Zoetrope") to produce moving animations on a [screen](/wiki/Projection_screen "Projection screen").[[18]](#cite_note-Cohen-18) The first of these, the [light-gun game](/wiki/Light_gun_shooter "Light gun shooter") *Duck Hunt*,[[19]](#cite_note-Duck-19) appeared in 1969;[[20]](#cite_note-20) it featured animated moving targets on a screen, printed out the player's [score](/wiki/Score_(game) "Score (game)") on a ticket, and had volume-controllable sound-effects.[[19]](#cite_note-Duck-19) Another Sega 1969 release, *Missile*, a [shooter](/wiki/Shooter_game "Shooter game"), featured electronic sound and a moving film strip to represent the targets on a projection screen.[[21]](#cite_note-Missile-21)

### 1970s to early 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 1970s to early 1980s")]

See also: [Golden age of arcade video games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games") and [First generation of video game consoles](/wiki/First_generation_of_video_game_consoles "First generation of video game consoles")

The first [arcade video game](/wiki/Arcade_video_game "Arcade video game"), [Atari, Inc.](/wiki/Atari,_Inc. "Atari, Inc.")'s *[Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong")*, debuted in the United States in 1972, and led to a number of new American manufacturers to create their own arcade games to capitalize on the rising fad. Several of these companies had Japanese partners and kept their overseas counterparts abreast of this new technology, leading several Japanese coin-operated electronic games makers to step into the arcade game market as well.[[22]](#cite_note-gamestudies_geemu-22) [Taito](/wiki/Taito "Taito") and [Namco](/wiki/Namco "Namco") were some of the early adopters of arcade games in Japan, first distributing American games before developing their own. [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") which at this time was primarily manufacturing traditional and electronic toys, also entered the arcade game market in the latter part of the 1970s.[[22]](#cite_note-gamestudies_geemu-22)

As in the United States, many of the early Japanese arcade games were based on the principle of [cloning](/wiki/Video_game_clone "Video game clone") gameplay established by popular titles to make new ones.[[22]](#cite_note-gamestudies_geemu-22) However, several new concepts came out of these Japanese-developed games, and performed well in both Japan and in re-licensed versions in the United States, such as Taito's *[Speed Race](/wiki/Speed_Race "Speed Race")* and *[Gun Fight](/wiki/Gun_Fight "Gun Fight")* in 1975. *Gun Fight* notably, when released by [Midway Games](/wiki/Midway_Games "Midway Games") in the U.S., was the first arcade game to use a [microprocessor](/wiki/Microprocessor "Microprocessor") rather than discrete electronic components.[[22]](#cite_note-gamestudies_geemu-22) [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega")'s black and white boxing game *[Heavyweight Champ](/wiki/Heavyweight_Champ "Heavyweight Champ")* was released in 1976 as the first video game to feature [fist fighting](/wiki/Fighting_game "Fighting game").[[23]](#cite_note-23) The first [stealth games](/wiki/Stealth_game "Stealth game") were Hiroshi Suzuki's *Manbiki Shounen* (1979)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) and *Manbiki Shoujo* (1980), [Taito](/wiki/Taito "Taito")'s *[Lupin III](/wiki/List_of_Lupin_III_video_games "List of Lupin III video games")* (1980),[[26]](#cite_note-26) and [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega")'s *[005](/wiki/005_(video_game) "005 (video game)")* (1981).[[27]](#cite_note-Popularplay-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/TV_Tennis_Electrotennis.jpg/250px-TV_Tennis_Electrotennis.jpg)](/wiki/File:TV_Tennis_Electrotennis.jpg)

[Epoch](/wiki/Epoch_Co. "Epoch Co.")'s TV Tennis Electrotennis, Japan's first home video game console

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Nintendo-Color-TV-Game-Blockbreaker-FL.jpg/250px-Nintendo-Color-TV-Game-Blockbreaker-FL.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nintendo-Color-TV-Game-Blockbreaker-FL.jpg)

The [Color TV-Game](/wiki/Color_TV-Game "Color TV-Game") Block Kuzushi

Separately, the first home video game console, the [Magnavox Odyssey](/wiki/Magnavox_Odyssey "Magnavox Odyssey"), had been released in the U.S. in 1971, of which Nintendo had partnered to manufacture the [light gun](/wiki/Light_gun "Light gun") accessory for the console, while Atari began releasing home console versions of *Pong* in 1975. Japan's first home video game console was [Epoch](/wiki/Epoch_Co. "Epoch Co.")'s [TV Tennis Electrotennis](/wiki/First_generation_of_video_game_consoles "First generation of video game consoles"). It was followed by the first successful Japanese console, Nintendo's [Color TV-Game](/wiki/Color_TV-Game "Color TV-Game"), in 1977 which was made in partnership with [Mitsubishi Electronics](/wiki/Mitsubishi_Electronics "Mitsubishi Electronics"). Numerous other dedicated home consoles were made mostly by television manufacturers, leading these systems to be called *TV geemu* or *terebi geemu* in Japan.[[22]](#cite_note-gamestudies_geemu-22)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Tomohiro-nishikado.jpg/250px-Tomohiro-nishikado.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tomohiro-nishikado.jpg)

[Tomohiro Nishikado](/wiki/Tomohiro_Nishikado "Tomohiro Nishikado"), creator of the [shooter game](/wiki/Shooter_game "Shooter game") *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")*

Eventually, the 1978 arcade release of *Space Invaders* would mark the first major mainstream breakthrough for video games in Japan.[[30]](#cite_note-jg-30) Created by Nishikado at Japan's [Taito](/wiki/Taito "Taito"), *Space Invaders* pitted the player against multiple enemies descending from the top of the screen at a constantly increasing speed.[[31]](#cite_note-1UP-10things-31)[[32]](#cite_note-Space_Invaders-32) The game used alien creatures inspired by *[The War of the Worlds](/wiki/The_War_of_the_Worlds "The War of the Worlds")* (by [H. G. Wells](/wiki/H._G._Wells "H. G. Wells")) because the developers were unable to render the movement of aircraft; in turn, the aliens replaced human enemies because of moral concerns (regarding the portrayal of killing humans) on the part of Taito. As with subsequent [shoot 'em ups](/wiki/Shoot_%27em_up "Shoot 'em up") of the time, the game was set in space as the available technology only permitted a black background. The game also introduced the idea of giving the player a number of "[lives](/wiki/Life_bar "Life bar")". It popularised a more interactive style of gameplay with the enemies responding to the player-controlled cannon's movement,[[33]](#cite_note-RG-3-33) and it was the first video game to popularise the concept of achieving a [high score](/wiki/Score_(game) "Score (game)"),[[34]](#cite_note-IGN-10-34)[[35]](#cite_note-GS-SI-Hall-35)[[36]](#cite_note-GWR08-2-36) being the first to [save](/wiki/Saved_game "Saved game") the player's score.[[34]](#cite_note-IGN-10-34) The aliens of *Space Invaders* return fire at the protagonist, making them the first arcade game targets to do so.[[37]](#cite_note-37) It set the template for the shoot 'em up genre,[[38]](#cite_note-1UP-50-38) and has influenced most shooting games released since then.[[31]](#cite_note-1UP-10things-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Donkey_Kong_arcade_at_the_QuakeCon_2005.png/250px-Donkey_Kong_arcade_at_the_QuakeCon_2005.png)](/wiki/File:Donkey_Kong_arcade_at_the_QuakeCon_2005.png)

*[Donkey Kong](/wiki/Donkey_Kong_(arcade_game) "Donkey Kong (arcade game)")* [arcade cabinet](/wiki/Arcade_cabinet "Arcade cabinet")

Taito's *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")*, in 1978, proved to be the first [blockbuster](/wiki/Blockbuster_(entertainment) "Blockbuster (entertainment)") arcade video game.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Its success marked the beginning of the [golden age of arcade video games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games"). Video game arcades sprang up in shopping malls, and small "corner arcades" appeared in restaurants, grocery stores, bars and movie theaters all over Japan and other countries during the late 1970s and early 1980s. *Space Invaders* (1978), *Galaxian* (1979), *[Pac-Man](/wiki/Pac-Man "Pac-Man")* (1980) and *[Bosconian](/wiki/Bosconian "Bosconian")* (1981) were especially popular. By 1981, the arcade video game industry was worth $8 billion[[40]](#cite_note-40) ($28.3 billion in 2025). Some games of this era were so popular that they entered [popular culture](/wiki/Popular_culture "Popular culture"). The first to do so was *Space Invaders*. The game was so popular upon its release in 1978 that an urban legend blamed it for a national shortage of [100 yen coins](/wiki/100_yen_coin "100 yen coin") in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), leading to a production increase of coins to meet demand for the game[[41]](#cite_note-GWR08-2a-41)[[42]](#cite_note-guardian-42) (although 100 yen coin production was lower in 1978 and 1979 than in previous or subsequent years,[[43]](#cite_note-coinguide-43)[[44]](#cite_note-WCN39-2-44) and the claim does not withstand logical scrutiny: arcade operators would have emptied out their machines and taken the money to the bank, thus keeping the coins in circulation).[[44]](#cite_note-WCN39-2-44) Japanese arcade games during the golden age also had hardware unit sales at least in the tens of thousands, including *[Ms. Pac-Man](/wiki/Ms._Pac-Man "Ms. Pac-Man")* with over 115,000 units,[[45]](#cite_note-Wolf-44-45) *[Donkey Kong](/wiki/Donkey_Kong_(arcade_game) "Donkey Kong (arcade game)")* with over 60,000,[[46]](#cite_note-Kent-352-46) *[Galaxian](/wiki/Galaxian "Galaxian")* with 40,000,[[47]](#cite_note-Bureau_of_National_Affairs_1983-47) *[Donkey Kong Jr.](/wiki/Donkey_Kong_Jr. "Donkey Kong Jr.")* with 35,000,[[46]](#cite_note-Kent-352-46) and *[Mr. Do!](/wiki/Mr._Do! "Mr. Do!")* with 30,000.[[48]](#cite_note-Steve_L._Kent_2001_352-48)

Other Japanese arcade games established new concepts that would become fundamentals in video games. Use of color graphics and individualized antagonists were considered "strong evolutionary concepts" among space ship games.[[49]](#cite_note-joystick_10-49) The Namco's *[Galaxian](/wiki/Galaxian "Galaxian")* in 1979 introduced multi-colored animated sprites.[[50]](#cite_note-Boost-50) That same year saw the release of [SNK](/wiki/SNK "SNK")'s debut shoot 'em up *[Ozma Wars](/wiki/Ozma_Wars "Ozma Wars")*, notable for being the first [action game](/wiki/Action_game "Action game") to feature a supply of energy, resembling a [life bar](/wiki/Health_(gaming)#Life_bar "Health (gaming)"), a mechanic that has now become common in the majority of modern action games.[[51]](#cite_note-51) It also featured vertically [scrolling](/wiki/Scrolling "Scrolling") backgrounds and enemies.[[52]](#cite_note-GameSpot-52)

### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 1980s")]

See also: [1980s in video games](/wiki/1980s_in_video_games "1980s in video games"), [Third generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Third_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Third generation of video game consoles"), and [Fourth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Fourth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Fourth generation of video game consoles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Nintendo-Famicom-Console-Set-FL.jpg/250px-Nintendo-Famicom-Console-Set-FL.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nintendo-Famicom-Console-Set-FL.jpg)

The [Family Computer](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System") (commonly known by the [Japanese-English](/wiki/Wasei-eigo "Wasei-eigo") term Famicom)

From [1980 to 1991](/wiki/1980s_in_video_gaming "1980s in video gaming"), Nintendo produced a line of [handheld electronic games](/wiki/Handheld_electronic_game "Handheld electronic game") called *[Game & Watch](/wiki/Game_%26_Watch "Game & Watch")*. Created by game designer [Gunpei Yokoi](/wiki/Gunpei_Yokoi "Gunpei Yokoi"), each *Game & Watch* features a single game to be played on an [LCD](/wiki/Liquid-crystal_display "Liquid-crystal display") screen. It was the earliest Nintendo product to gain major success.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Mega Man, known as Rockman (ロックマン, Rokkuman) in Japan, is a Japanese science fiction video game franchise created by Capcom, starring a series of robot characters each known by the moniker "Mega Man". Mega Man, released for the Nintendo Entertainment System in 1987, was the first in a series that expanded to over 50 games on multiple systems. As of March 31, 2021, the game series has sold 36 million units worldwide.[1]

[Konami](/wiki/Konami "Konami")'s *[Scramble](/wiki/Scramble_(arcade_game) "Scramble (arcade game)")*, released in 1981, is a [side-scrolling](/wiki/Side-scrolling_video_game "Side-scrolling video game") shooter with forced scrolling. It was the first scrolling shooter to offer multiple, distinct [levels](/wiki/Level_(video_gaming) "Level (video gaming)").[[54]](#cite_note-lecture-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) Vertical scrolling shooters emerged around the same time. Namco's *[Xevious](/wiki/Xevious "Xevious")*, released in 1982, is frequently cited as the first vertical scrolling shooter and, although it was in fact preceded by several other games of that type, it is considered one of the most influential.[[54]](#cite_note-lecture-54)

The first [platform game](/wiki/Platform_game "Platform game") to use [scrolling](/wiki/Scrolling "Scrolling") graphics was *[Jump Bug](/wiki/Jump_Bug "Jump Bug")* (1981), a simple platform-[shooter game](/wiki/Shooter_game "Shooter game") developed by [Alpha Denshi](/wiki/Alpha_Denshi "Alpha Denshi").[[56]](#cite_note-56)

The North American video game industry was devastated by the [1983 video game crash](/wiki/1983_video_game_crash "1983 video game crash"), but in Japan, it was more of a surprise to developers, and typically known in Japan as the "Atari Shock". After the video game crash, analysts doubted the long-term viability of the video game industry.[[57]](#cite_note-57) At the same time, following a series of [arcade game](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") successes in the early 1980s, Nintendo made plans to create a cartridge-based console called the Famicom, which is short for Family Computer. Masayuki Uemura designed the system.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) The console was released on July 15, 1983, as the Family Computer (or Famicom for short) alongside three [ports](/wiki/Porting "Porting") of Nintendo's successful arcade games *[Donkey Kong](/wiki/Donkey_Kong_(arcade_game) "Donkey Kong (arcade game)")*, *[Donkey Kong Jr.](/wiki/Donkey_Kong_Jr. "Donkey Kong Jr.")* and *[Popeye](/wiki/Popeye_(video_game) "Popeye (video game)")*. The Famicom was slow to gather momentum; a bad chip set caused the initial release of the system to [crash](/wiki/Crash_(computing) "Crash (computing)"). Following a [product recall](/wiki/Product_recall "Product recall") and a reissue with a new [motherboard](/wiki/Motherboard "Motherboard"), the Famicom's popularity soared, becoming the best-selling game console in Japan by the end of 1984.[[60]](#cite_note-60) By 1988, industry observers stated that the NES's popularity had grown so quickly that the market for Nintendo cartridges was larger than that for all home computer software.[[61]](#cite_note-cgw198806-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62) By mid-1986, 19% (6.5 million) of Japanese households owned a Famicom;[[63]](#cite_note-takiff19860620a-63) one third by mid-1988.[[64]](#cite_note-keiser198806-64) In June 1989, Nintendo of America's vice president of marketing Peter Main, said that the Famicom was present in 37% of Japan's households.[[65]](#cite_note-How_Nintendo_Can_Help_A.T.&T-65) By the end of its run, over 60 million NES units had been sold throughout the world.[[66]](#cite_note-sales-66) In 1990 Nintendo surpassed [Toyota](/wiki/Toyota "Toyota") as Japan's most successful corporation.[[67]](#cite_note-liedholm3-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)

Because the NES was released after the "video game crash" of the early 1980s, many retailers and adults regarded electronic games as a passing fad,[[69]](#cite_note-69) so many believed at first that the NES would soon fade.[[70]](#cite_note-keizer198907-70) Before the NES/Famicom, Nintendo was known as a moderately successful Japanese toy and playing card manufacturer, but the popularity of the NES/Famicom helped the company grow into an internationally recognized name almost synonymous with video games as Atari had been,[[71]](#cite_note-71) and set the stage for Japanese dominance of the video game industry.[[72]](#cite_note-72) With the NES, Nintendo also changed the relationship between [console manufacturers](/wiki/Console_manufacturer "Console manufacturer") and third-party software developers by restricting developers from publishing and distributing software without licensed approval. This led to higher quality software titles, which helped change the attitude of a public that had grown weary from poorly produced titles for earlier game systems.[[73]](#cite_note-Genesisn't-73) The system's hardware limitations led to design principles that still influence the development of modern video games. Many prominent game franchises originated on the NES, including Nintendo's own *[Super Mario Bros.](/wiki/Mario_(franchise) "Mario (franchise)")*,[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKohler200557-74) *[The Legend of Zelda](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda "The Legend of Zelda")*[[75]](#cite_note-75) and *[Metroid](/wiki/Metroid "Metroid")*,[[76]](#cite_note-76) [Capcom](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom")'s *[Mega Man](/wiki/Mega_Man_(original_series) "Mega Man (original series)")*[[77]](#cite_note-77) franchise, [Konami](/wiki/Konami "Konami")'s *[Castlevania](/wiki/Castlevania "Castlevania")*[[78]](#cite_note-78) franchise, [Square](/wiki/Square_(video_game_company) "Square (video game company)")'s *[Final Fantasy](/wiki/Final_Fantasy "Final Fantasy")*,[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKohler200595-79) and [Enix](/wiki/Enix "Enix")'s *[Dragon Quest](/wiki/Dragon_Quest "Dragon Quest")* franchises.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKohler2005222-80)

Following the release of the Family Computer / Nintendo Entertainment System, the global video game industry began recovering, with annual sales exceeding $2.3 billion by 1988, with 70% of the market dominated by Nintendo.[[81]](#cite_note-81) In 1986 Nintendo president [Hiroshi Yamauchi](/wiki/Hiroshi_Yamauchi "Hiroshi Yamauchi") noted that "Atari collapsed because they gave too much freedom to third-party developers and the market was swamped with rubbish games". In response, Nintendo limited the number of titles that third-party developers could release for their system each year, and promoted its "[Seal of Quality](/wiki/Nintendo#Seal_of_Quality "Nintendo")", which it allowed to be used on games and peripherals by publishers that met Nintendo's quality standards.[[82]](#cite_note-takiff19860620-82)

Japan's first [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") for gaming soon appeared, the [Sord M200](/wiki/Sord_Computer_Corporation "Sord Computer Corporation") in 1977 and [Sharp MZ-80K](/wiki/Sharp_MZ "Sharp MZ") in 1978.[[30]](#cite_note-jg-30) In Japan, both consoles and computers became major industries, with the console market dominated by Nintendo and the computer market dominated by [NEC](/wiki/NEC "NEC")'s [PC-88](/wiki/NEC_PC-8801 "NEC PC-8801") (1981) and [PC-98](/wiki/NEC_PC-9801 "NEC PC-9801") (1982). A key difference between Western and Japanese computers at the time was the [display resolution](/wiki/Display_resolution "Display resolution"), with Japanese systems using a higher resolution of 640x400 to accommodate [Japanese text](/wiki/Japanese_writing_system "Japanese writing system") which in turn affected [video game design](/wiki/Video_game_design "Video game design") and allowed more detailed graphics. Japanese computers were also using [Yamaha](/wiki/Yamaha_Corporation "Yamaha Corporation")'s [FM synth](/wiki/Frequency_modulation_synthesis "Frequency modulation synthesis") [sound boards](/wiki/Sound_card "Sound card") from the early 1980s.[[83]](#cite_note-Retro-83) During the [16-bit era](/wiki/Fourth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Fourth generation of video game consoles"), the PC-98, [Sharp X68000](/wiki/X68000 "X68000") and [FM Towns](/wiki/FM_Towns "FM Towns") became popular in Japan. The X68000 and FM Towns were capable of producing near [arcade](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game")-quality hardware [sprite](/wiki/Sprite_(computer_graphics) "Sprite (computer graphics)") graphics and sound quality when they first released in the mid-to-late 1980s.[[83]](#cite_note-Retro-83)

The *[Wizardry](/wiki/Wizardry_(video_game_series) "Wizardry (video game series)")* series (translated by [ASCII Entertainment](/wiki/ASCII_Entertainment "ASCII Entertainment")) became popular and influential in Japan, even more so than at home.[[84]](#cite_note-maher20140625-84) Japanese developers created the [action RPG](/wiki/Action_RPG "Action RPG") subgenre in the early 1980s, combining [RPG](/wiki/Role-playing_video_game "Role-playing video game") elements with [arcade](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game")-style [action](/wiki/Action_game "Action game") and [action-adventure](/wiki/Action-adventure_game "Action-adventure game") elements.[[85]](#cite_note-cgw_76-85)[[86]](#cite_note-1up_parish-86) The trend of combining role-playing elements with arcade-style action mechanics was popularized by *[The Tower of Druaga](/wiki/The_Tower_of_Druaga "The Tower of Druaga")*,[[86]](#cite_note-1up_parish-86) an [arcade game](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") released by [Namco](/wiki/Namco "Namco") in June 1984. While the RPG elements in *Druaga* were very subtle, its success in Japan inspired the near-simultaneous development of three early [action role-playing games](/wiki/Action_role-playing_game "Action role-playing game"), combining *Druaga*'s real-time [hack-and-slash](/wiki/Hack-and-slash "Hack-and-slash") gameplay with stronger RPG mechanics, all released in late 1984: *[Dragon Slayer](/wiki/Dragon_Slayer_(video_game) "Dragon Slayer (video game)")*, *Courageous Perseus*, and *[Hydlide](/wiki/Hydlide "Hydlide")*. A rivalry developed between the three games, with *Dragon Slayer* and *Hydlide* continuing their rivalry through subsequent sequels.[[87]](#cite_note-87) *The Tower of Druaga*, *Dragon Slayer* and *Hydlide* were influential in Japan, where they laid the foundations for the action RPG genre, influencing titles such as *[Ys](/wiki/Ys_(series) "Ys (series)")* and *[The Legend of Zelda](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda "The Legend of Zelda")*.[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-dragon_slayer-89)

The [action role-playing game](/wiki/Action_role-playing_game "Action role-playing game") *[Hydlide](/wiki/Hydlide "Hydlide")* (1984) was an early open world game,[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-gamasutra-91) rewarding exploration in an open world environment.[[92]](#cite_note-92) *Hydlide* influenced *[The Legend of Zelda](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda_(video_game) "The Legend of Zelda (video game)")* (1986),[[93]](#cite_note-Szczepaniak-93) an influential open world game.[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95) *Zelda* had an expansive, coherent open world design, inspiring many games to adopt a similar open world design.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

*[Bokosuka Wars](/wiki/Bokosuka_Wars "Bokosuka Wars")* (1983) is considered an early prototype [real-time strategy](/wiki/Real-time_strategy "Real-time strategy") game.[[97]](#cite_note-1UP-Druaga-97) [TechnoSoft](/wiki/TechnoSoft "TechnoSoft")'s *[Herzog](/wiki/Herzog_(video_game) "Herzog (video game)")* (1988) is regarded as a precursor to the real-time strategy genre, being the predecessor to *Herzog Zwei* and somewhat similar in nature.[[98]](#cite_note-98) *[Herzog Zwei](/wiki/Herzog_Zwei "Herzog Zwei")*, released for the [Sega Mega Drive/Genesis](/wiki/Mega_Drive "Mega Drive") [home console](/wiki/Home_console "Home console") in 1989, is the earliest example of a game with a feature set that falls under the contemporary definition of modern real-time strategy.[[99]](#cite_note-hzweifirstrts-99)[[100]](#cite_note-zzap-100)

[Data East](/wiki/Data_East "Data East")'s *[Karate Champ](/wiki/Karate_Champ "Karate Champ")* from 1984 is credited with establishing and popularizing the one-on-one [fighting game](/wiki/Fighting_game "Fighting game") genre, and went on to influence [Konami](/wiki/Konami "Konami")'s *[Yie Ar Kung-Fu](/wiki/Yie_Ar_Kung-Fu "Yie Ar Kung-Fu")* from 1985.[[101]](#cite_note-games.ign.com-101) [Capcom](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom")'s *[Street Fighter](/wiki/Street_Fighter "Street Fighter")* (1987) introduced the use of special moves that could only be discovered by experimenting with the game controls. *[Street Fighter II](/wiki/Street_Fighter_II "Street Fighter II")* (1991) established the conventions of the fighting game genre and allowed players to play against each other.[[102]](#cite_note-sfhistory-102)

In 1985, [Sega AM2](/wiki/Sega_AM2 "Sega AM2")'s *[Hang-On](/wiki/Hang-On "Hang-On")*, designed by [Yu Suzuki](/wiki/Yu_Suzuki "Yu Suzuki") and running on the [Sega Space Harrier](/wiki/Sega_Space_Harrier "Sega Space Harrier") hardware, was the first of Sega's "[Super Scaler](/wiki/Sega_Super_Scaler "Sega Super Scaler")" [arcade system boards](/wiki/Arcade_system_board "Arcade system board") that allowed [pseudo-3D](/wiki/2.5D "2.5D") [sprite-scaling](/wiki/Sprite_(computer_graphics)#Move_to_3D "Sprite (computer graphics)") at high [frame rates](/wiki/Frame_rate "Frame rate").[[103]](#cite_note-103) The pseudo-3D [sprite](/wiki/Sprite_(computer_graphics) "Sprite (computer graphics)")/[tile](/wiki/Tile_engine "Tile engine") scaling was handled in a similar manner to textures in later [texture-mapped](/wiki/Texture_mapping "Texture mapping") [polygonal 3D](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics") games of the 1990s.[[104]](#cite_note-pseudo-104) Designed by [Sega AM2](/wiki/Sega_AM2 "Sega AM2")'s [Yu Suzuki](/wiki/Yu_Suzuki "Yu Suzuki"), he stated that his "designs were always 3D from the beginning. All the calculations in the system were 3D, even from Hang-On. I calculated the position, scale, and zoom rate in 3D and converted it backwards to [2D](/wiki/2D_computer_graphics "2D computer graphics"). So I was always thinking in 3D."[[105]](#cite_note-suzuki-105) It was controlled using a [video game arcade cabinet](/wiki/Video_game_arcade_cabinet "Video game arcade cabinet") resembling a [motorbike](/wiki/Motorbike "Motorbike"), which the player moves with their body. This began the "Taikan" trend, the use of [motion-controlled](/wiki/Motion_controller "Motion controller") hydraulic arcade cabinets in many arcade games of the late 1980s, two decades before motion controls became popular on [video game consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console").[[106]](#cite_note-106)

Sega's *[Space Harrier](/wiki/Space_Harrier "Space Harrier")*, a rail shooter released in 1985, broke new ground graphically and its wide variety of settings across multiple levels gave players more to aim for than high scores.[[107]](#cite_note-107)[[108]](#cite_note-1upharrier-108) 1985 also saw the release of Konami's *[Gradius](/wiki/Gradius "Gradius")*, which gave the player greater control over the choice of weaponry, thus introducing another element of strategy.[[54]](#cite_note-lecture-54) The game also introduced the need for the player to memorise levels in order to achieve any measure of success.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAshcraft200876-109) *Gradius*, with its iconic protagonist, defined the side-scrolling shoot 'em up and spawned a series spanning several sequels.[[110]](#cite_note-110) The following year saw the emergence of one of Sega's forefront series with its game *[Fantasy Zone](/wiki/Fantasy_Zone "Fantasy Zone")*. The game received acclaim for its surreal graphics and setting and the protagonist, Opa-Opa, was for a time considered Sega's [mascot](/wiki/Mascot "Mascot").[[111]](#cite_note-111) The game borrowed *Defender's* device of allowing the player to control the direction of flight and along with the earlier *[TwinBee](/wiki/TwinBee "TwinBee")* (1985), is an early archetype of the "cute 'em up" subgenre.[[54]](#cite_note-lecture-54)[[112]](#cite_note-112)

*[Hydlide II: Shine of Darkness](/wiki/Hydlide_II:_Shine_of_Darkness "Hydlide II: Shine of Darkness")* in 1985 featured an early morality meter, where the player can be [aligned](/wiki/Alignment_(role-playing_games) "Alignment (role-playing games)") with justice, normal, or evil, which is affected by whether the player kills evil monsters, good monsters, or humans, and in turn affects the reactions of the townsfolk towards the player.[[113]](#cite_note-kalata_hydlide-113) In the same year, [Yuji Horii](/wiki/Yuji_Horii "Yuji Horii") and his team at [Chunsoft](/wiki/Chunsoft "Chunsoft") began production on *[Dragon Quest](/wiki/Dragon_Warrior "Dragon Warrior")* (*Dragon Warrior*).[[114]](#cite_note-1up_dw-114) After [Enix](/wiki/Enix "Enix") published the game in early 1986, it became the template for future console RPGs.[[115]](#cite_note-gspy_dwhof-115) Horii's intention behind *Dragon Quest* was to create a RPG that appeals to a wider audience unfamiliar with the genre or video games in general. This required the creation of a new kind of RPG, that didn't rely on previous *D&D* experience, didn't require [hundreds of hours of rote fighting](/wiki/Grinding_(video_gaming) "Grinding (video gaming)"), and that could appeal to any kind of gamer.[[114]](#cite_note-1up_dw-114) The streamlined gameplay of *Dragon Quest* thus made the game more accessible to a wider audience than previous computer RPGs.[[116]](#cite_note-1up_dq-116) The game also placed a greater emphasis on storytelling and emotional involvement,[[117]](#cite_note-npinterview-117) building on Horii's previous work *Portopia Serial Murder Case*, but this time introducing a [coming of age](/wiki/Coming_of_age "Coming of age") tale for *Dragon Quest* that audiences could relate to, making use of the RPG level-building gameplay as a way to represent this.[[118]](#cite_note-gotemba_iwamoto-118) It also featured elements still found in most console RPGs, like major quests interwoven with minor subquests, an incremental spell system,[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVestal1998"Dragon_Quest"-119) the [damsel-in-distress](/wiki/Damsel-in-distress "Damsel-in-distress") storyline that many RPGs follow,[[120]](#cite_note-120) and a romance element that remains a staple of the genre,[[121]](#cite_note-121) alongside [anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime")-style art by [Akira Toriyama](/wiki/Akira_Toriyama "Akira Toriyama") and a [classical](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music") score by [Koichi Sugiyama](/wiki/Koichi_Sugiyama "Koichi Sugiyama") that was considered revolutionary for console [video game music](/wiki/Video_game_music "Video game music").[[114]](#cite_note-1up_dw-114) With *Dragon Quest* becoming widely popular in Japan, such that local municipalities were forced to place restrictions on where and when the game could be sold,[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVestal1998"Dragon_Quest"-119) the [*Dragon Quest* series](/wiki/Dragon_Quest "Dragon Quest") is still considered a bellwether for the Japanese video game market.[[122]](#cite_note-1up_dqbell-122)

Shoot 'em ups featuring characters on foot, rather than spacecraft, became popular in the mid-1980s in the wake of [action movies](/wiki/Action_movie "Action movie") such as *[Rambo: First Blood Part II](/wiki/Rambo:_First_Blood_Part_II "Rambo: First Blood Part II").*[[52]](#cite_note-GameSpot-52) The origins of this type go back to *[Sheriff](/wiki/Sheriff_(video_game) "Sheriff (video game)")* by Nintendo, released in 1979. Taito's *[Front Line](/wiki/Front_Line_(video_game) "Front Line (video game)")* (1982) established the upwards-scrolling formula later popularized by [Capcom](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom")'s *[Commando](/wiki/Commando_(video_game) "Commando (video game)"),* in 1985, and [SNK](/wiki/SNK_Playmore "SNK Playmore")'s *[Ikari Warriors](/wiki/Ikari_Warriors "Ikari Warriors")* (1986).[[123]](#cite_note-ysguide2-123) *Commando* also drew comparisons to [Rambo](/wiki/Rambo_(film_series) "Rambo (film series)")[[124]](#cite_note-124) and indeed contemporary critics considered military themes and protagonists similar to Rambo or [Schwarzenegger](/wiki/Arnold_Schwarzenegger "Arnold Schwarzenegger") prerequisites for a shoot 'em up, as opposed to an [action-adventure game](/wiki/Action-adventure_game "Action-adventure game").[[123]](#cite_note-ysguide2-123) In 1986, [Arsys Software](/wiki/Arsys_Software "Arsys Software") released *[WiBArm](/wiki/Wibarm "Wibarm")*, a shooter that switched between a [2D](/wiki/2D_computer_graphics "2D computer graphics") side-scrolling view in outdoor areas to a fully 3D polygonal [third-person](/wiki/Third-person_(video_games) "Third-person (video games)") perspective inside buildings, while bosses were fought in an arena-style 2D battle, with the game featuring a variety of weapons and equipment.[[125]](#cite_note-retro_4-125)

The late 1980s to early 1990s is considered the golden age of Japanese computer gaming, which would flourish until its decline around the mid-1990s, as consoles eventually dominated the Japanese market.[[126]](#cite_note-retro_3-126) A notable Japanese computer RPG from around this time was *[WiBArm](/wiki/Wibarm "Wibarm")*, the earliest known RPG to feature [3D polygonal graphics](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics"). It was a 1986 role-playing shooter released by [Arsys Software](/wiki/Arsys_Software "Arsys Software") for the [PC-88](/wiki/PC-88 "PC-88") in Japan and ported to [MS-DOS](/wiki/MS-DOS "MS-DOS") for Western release by [Broderbund](/wiki/Broderbund "Broderbund"). In *WiBArm*, the player controls a transformable [mecha](/wiki/Mecha "Mecha") robot, switching between a [2D](/wiki/2D_computer_graphics "2D computer graphics") [side-scrolling](/wiki/Side-scrolling_video_game "Side-scrolling video game") view during outdoor exploration to a fully 3D polygonal [third-person](/wiki/Third-person_(video_games) "Third-person (video games)") perspective inside buildings, while bosses are fought in an arena-style 2D [shoot 'em up](/wiki/Shoot_%27em_up "Shoot 'em up") battle. The game featured a variety of weapons and equipment as well as an [automap](/wiki/Automap "Automap"), and the player could upgrade equipment and earn experience to raise stats.[[83]](#cite_note-Retro-83)[[127]](#cite_note-4gamer_wibarm-127) Unlike first-person RPGs at the time that were restricted to 90-degree movements, *WiBArm's* use of 3D polygons allowed full 360-degree movement.[[127]](#cite_note-4gamer_wibarm-127)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/PC-Engine-Console-Set.jpg/250px-PC-Engine-Console-Set.jpg)](/wiki/File:PC-Engine-Console-Set.jpg)

The [PC Engine](/wiki/PC_Engine "PC Engine"), released in 1987

On October 30, 1987, the [PC Engine](/wiki/PC_Engine "PC Engine") made its debut in the Japanese market and it was a tremendous success. The console had an elegant, "eye-catching" design, and it was very small compared to its rivals.[[128]](#cite_note-Gamasutra-128) The PC Engine, TurboGrafx-16, known as TurboGrafx-16 in the rest of the world, was a collaborative effort between [Hudson Soft](/wiki/Hudson_Soft "Hudson Soft"), who created video game software, and [NEC](/wiki/NEC "NEC"), a major company which was dominant in the Japanese [personal computer](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") market with their [PC-88](/wiki/PC-88 "PC-88") and [PC-98](/wiki/PC-98 "PC-98") platforms.[[129]](#cite_note-129)

*[R-Type](/wiki/R-Type "R-Type")*, an acclaimed side-scrolling shoot 'em up, was released in 1987 by [Irem](/wiki/Irem "Irem"), employing slower-paced scrolling than usual, with difficult levels calling for methodical strategies.[[130]](#cite_note-igntop10-130)[[131]](#cite_note-131) 1990's *[Raiden](/wiki/Raiden_(arcade_game) "Raiden (arcade game)")* was the beginning of another acclaimed and enduring series to emerge from this period.[[132]](#cite_note-132)[[133]](#cite_note-133) In 1987, Square's *[3-D WorldRunner](/wiki/3-D_WorldRunner "3-D WorldRunner")* was an early stereoscopic 3-D shooter played from a third-person perspective,[[134]](#cite_note-134) followed later that year by its sequel *[JJ](/wiki/JJ_(video_game) "JJ (video game)")*,[[135]](#cite_note-135) and the following year by *[Space Harrier 3-D](/wiki/Space_Harrier_3-D "Space Harrier 3-D")* which used the SegaScope [3-D shutter glasses](/wiki/Liquid_crystal_shutter_glasses "Liquid crystal shutter glasses").[[136]](#cite_note-136) Also in 1987, Konami created *[Contra](/wiki/Contra_(video_game) "Contra (video game)")* as a coin-op arcade game that was particularly acclaimed for its multi-directional aiming and two player cooperative gameplay.

*[Digital Devil Story: Megami Tensei](/wiki/Digital_Devil_Story:_Megami_Tensei "Digital Devil Story: Megami Tensei")* by [Atlus](/wiki/Atlus "Atlus") for the Nintendo Famicom abandoned the common medieval fantasy setting and [sword and sorcery](/wiki/Sword_and_sorcery "Sword and sorcery") theme in favour of a modern science-fiction setting and horror theme. It also introduced the [monster-catching](/wiki/Digital_pet "Digital pet") mechanic with its demon-summoning system, which allowed the player to recruit enemies into their party, through a conversation system that gives the player a choice of whether to kill or spare an enemy and allows them to engage any opponent in conversation.[[137]](#cite_note-HardcoreMegaten-137) [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega")'s [original *Phantasy Star*](/wiki/Phantasy_Star_(video_game) "Phantasy Star (video game)") for the [Master System](/wiki/Master_System "Master System") combined sci-fi & fantasy setting that set it apart from the *D&D* staple.[[138]](#cite_note-cvg_phantasy-138) It was also one of the first games to feature a female protagonist and animated monster encounters,[[138]](#cite_note-cvg_phantasy-138) and allowed inter-planetary travel between three planets.[[139]](#cite_note-139) Another 1987 title *[Miracle Warriors: Seal of the Dark Lord](/wiki/Miracle_Warriors:_Seal_of_the_Dark_Lord "Miracle Warriors: Seal of the Dark Lord")* was a [third-person](/wiki/Third-person_(video_games) "Third-person (video games)") RPG that featured a wide [open world](/wiki/Open_world "Open world") and a [mini-map](/wiki/Mini-map "Mini-map") on the corner of the screen.[[140]](#cite_note-140)

According to *[Wizardry](/wiki/Wizardry_(video_game_series) "Wizardry (video game series)")* developer Roe R. Adams, early [action-adventure games](/wiki/Action-adventure_games "Action-adventure games") "were basically arcade games done in a [fantasy](/wiki/Fantasy "Fantasy") setting," citing *[Castlevania](/wiki/Castlevania "Castlevania")* (1986) and *[Trojan](/wiki/Trojan_(video_game) "Trojan (video game)")* (1986) as examples.[[141]](#cite_note-141) [IGN UK](/wiki/IGN "IGN") argues that *[The Legend of Zelda](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda_(1986_video_game) "The Legend of Zelda (1986 video game)")* (1986) "helped to establish a new subgenre of action-adventure", becoming a success due to how it combined elements from different genres to create a compelling hybrid, including exploration, adventure-style inventory [puzzles](/wiki/Puzzle_game "Puzzle game"), an action component, a [monetary system](/wiki/Monetary_system "Monetary system"), and simplified RPG-style [level building](/wiki/Experience_point#Level-based_progression "Experience point") without the [experience points](/wiki/Experience_point "Experience point").[[142]](#cite_note-142) *[The Legend of Zelda](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda "The Legend of Zelda")* was the most prolific action-adventure game series through to the 2000s.[[143]](#cite_note-143)

The first [Nintendo Space World](/wiki/Nintendo_Space_World "Nintendo Space World") show was held on July 28, 1989.[[144]](#cite_note-144) It was a video game [trade show](/wiki/Trade_fair "Trade fair") that was hosted by Nintendo until 2001. At the same year, *[Phantasy Star II](/wiki/Phantasy_Star_II "Phantasy Star II")* for the [Genesis](/wiki/Mega_Drive "Mega Drive") established many conventions of the RPG genre, including an [epic](/wiki/Epic_(genre) "Epic (genre)"), dramatic, character-driven storyline dealing with serious themes and subject matter, and a strategy-based battle system.[[138]](#cite_note-cvg_phantasy-138)[[145]](#cite_note-gspot_phanstar2-145) The game's science fiction story was also unique, reversing the common [alien invasion](/wiki/Alien_invasion "Alien invasion") scenario by instead presenting [Earthlings](/wiki/Earth_in_science_fiction "Earth in science fiction") as the invading antagonists rather than the defending protagonists.[[138]](#cite_note-cvg_phantasy-138)[[145]](#cite_note-gspot_phanstar2-145) [Capcom](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom")'s *[Sweet Home](/wiki/Sweet_Home_(video_game) "Sweet Home (video game)")* for the NES introduced a modern [Japanese horror](/wiki/J-Horror "J-Horror") theme and laid the foundations for the [survival horror](/wiki/Survival_horror "Survival horror") genre, later serving as the main inspiration for *[Resident Evil](/wiki/Resident_Evil "Resident Evil")* (1996).[[146]](#cite_note-GameSpy-146)[[147]](#cite_note-gspy_resevil-147) *[Tengai Makyo: Ziria](/wiki/Tengai_Makyo "Tengai Makyo")* released for the [PC Engine CD](/wiki/PC_Engine_CD "PC Engine CD") that same year was the first RPG released on [CD-ROM](/wiki/CD-ROM "CD-ROM") and the first in the genre to feature animated [cut scenes](/wiki/Cut_scene "Cut scene") and voice acting. The game's plot was also unusual for its [feudal Japan](/wiki/Feudal_Japan "Feudal Japan") setting and its emphasis on humour; the plot and characters were inspired by the Japanese folk tale *[Jiraiya Goketsu Monogatari](/wiki/Jiraiya "Jiraiya")*. The music for the game was also composed by noted musician [Ryuichi Sakamoto](/wiki/Ryuichi_Sakamoto "Ryuichi Sakamoto").[[148]](#cite_note-148)

### 1990s to early 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 1990s to early 2000s")]

See also: [1990s in video games](/wiki/1990s_in_video_games "1990s in video games"), [Fourth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Fourth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Fourth generation of video game consoles"), [Fifth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Fifth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Fifth generation of video game consoles"), [Sixth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Sixth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Sixth generation of video game consoles"), and [2000s in video games](/wiki/2000s_in_video_games "2000s in video games")

The ‘golden age’ of console RPGs is often dated in the 1990s.[[149]](#cite_note-gradar_jrpgs-149)[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarton2008228-150) Console RPGs distinguished themselves from computer RPGs to a greater degree in the early 1990s. As console RPGs became more heavily story-based than their computer counterparts, one of the major differences that emerged during this time was in the portrayal of the characters, with most American computer RPGs at the time having characters devoid of personality or background as their purpose was to represent [avatars](/wiki/Avatar_(computing) "Avatar (computing)") which the player uses to interact with the world, in contrast to Japanese console RPGs which depicted pre-defined characters who had distinctive personalities, traits, and relationships, such as *[Final Fantasy](/wiki/Final_Fantasy "Final Fantasy")* and *[Lufia](/wiki/Lufia "Lufia")*, with players assuming the roles of people who cared about each other, fell in love or even had families. Romance in particular was a theme that was common in most console RPGs but alien to most computer RPGs at the time.[[151]](#cite_note-hallford_xxiv-151) Japanese console RPGs were also generally more faster-paced and [action-adventure](/wiki/Action-adventure_game "Action-adventure game")-oriented than their American computer counterparts.[[152]](#cite_note-cgw76_84-152)[[153]](#cite_note-joyq_evw-153) During the 1990s, console RPGs had become increasingly dominant.[[154]](#cite_note-154)

In 1990, *[Dragon Quest IV](/wiki/Dragon_Quest_IV:_Chapters_of_the_Chosen "Dragon Quest IV: Chapters of the Chosen")* introduced a new method of storytelling: segmenting the plot into segregated chapters.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVestal1998"Dragon_Quest_IV"-155)[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris20098-156) The game also introduced an [AI](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence_(video_games) "Artificial intelligence (video games)") system called "Tactics" which allowed the player to modify the strategies used by the allied party members while maintaining full control of the hero.[[157]](#cite_note-157) *[Final Fantasy III](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_III "Final Fantasy III")* introduced the classic "[job system](/wiki/Job_system "Job system")", a character progression engine allowing the player to change the [character classes](/wiki/Character_class "Character class"), as well as acquire new and advanced classes and combine class abilities, during the course of the game.[[158]](#cite_note-se_ff3-158)[[159]](#cite_note-square_ffmanual-159) That same year also saw the release of Nintendo's *[Fire Emblem: Ankoku Ryu to Hikari no Tsurugi](/wiki/Fire_Emblem:_Ankoku_Ryu_to_Hikari_no_Tsurugi "Fire Emblem: Ankoku Ryu to Hikari no Tsurugi")*, a game that set the template for the [tactical role-playing game](/wiki/Tactical_role-playing_game "Tactical role-playing game") genre and was the first entry in the *[Fire Emblem](/wiki/Fire_Emblem "Fire Emblem")* series.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris200914-160) Another notable strategy RPG that year was [Koei](/wiki/Koei "Koei")'s *[Bandit Kings of Ancient China](/wiki/Bandit_Kings_of_Ancient_China "Bandit Kings of Ancient China")*, which was successful in combining the strategy RPG and [management simulation](/wiki/Construction_and_management_simulation "Construction and management simulation") genres, building on its own *[Nobunaga's Ambition](/wiki/Nobunaga%27s_Ambition "Nobunaga's Ambition")* series that began in 1983.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVestal1998"Dragon_Quest_IV"-155) Several early RPGs set in a [post-apocalyptic](/wiki/Apocalyptic_and_post-apocalyptic_fiction "Apocalyptic and post-apocalyptic fiction") future were also released that year, including *[Digital Devil Story: Megami Tensei II](/wiki/Digital_Devil_Story:_Megami_Tensei_II "Digital Devil Story: Megami Tensei II")*,[[137]](#cite_note-HardcoreMegaten-137)[[161]](#cite_note-GamaList-161) and *[Crystalis](/wiki/Crystalis "Crystalis")*,[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVestal1998"Crystalis"-162) which was inspired by Hayao Miyazaki's *[Nausicaa of the Valley of the Wind](/wiki/Nausica%C3%A4_of_the_Valley_of_the_Wind_(film) "Nausicaä of the Valley of the Wind (film)")*. *Crystalis* also made advances to the action role-playing game subgenre, being a true action RPG that combined the real-time [action-adventure](/wiki/Action-adventure_game "Action-adventure game") combat and [open world](/wiki/Open_world "Open world") of *[The Legend of Zelda](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda_(1986_video_game) "The Legend of Zelda (1986 video game)")* with the level-building and spell-casting of traditional RPGs like *Final Fantasy*.[[163]](#cite_note-1up_console_vs_handheld-163) That year also saw the release of *[Phantasy Star III: Generations of Doom](/wiki/Phantasy_Star_III:_Generations_of_Doom "Phantasy Star III: Generations of Doom")*, which featured an innovative and original branching storyline, which spans three generations of characters and can be altered depending on which character the protagonist of each generation marries,[[164]](#cite_note-164) leading to four possible endings.[[138]](#cite_note-cvg_phantasy-138)

In 1991, *[Final Fantasy IV](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_IV "Final Fantasy IV")* was one of the first role-playing games to feature a complex, involving plot,[[165]](#cite_note-rpgfan_ffii-165) placing a much greater emphasis on character development, personal relationships, and dramatic storytelling.[[166]](#cite_note-gspot_ffiv-166) It also introduced a new [battle system](/wiki/Role-playing_battle_systems "Role-playing battle systems"): the "[Active Time Battle](/wiki/Active_Time_Battle "Active Time Battle")" system, developed by [Hiroyuki Ito](/wiki/Hiroyuki_Ito "Hiroyuki Ito"),[[167]](#cite_note-gt_ff_retrospective_xiii-167) where the [time-keeping system](/wiki/Time-keeping_systems_in_games "Time-keeping systems in games") does not stop.[[168]](#cite_note-Vestal-IV-168) The fact that enemies can attack or be attacked at any time is credited with injecting urgency and excitement into the combat system.[[168]](#cite_note-Vestal-IV-168) The ATB combat system was considered revolutionary for being a hybrid between [turn-based](/wiki/Turn-based_game "Turn-based game") and [real-time](/wiki/Real-time_game "Real-time game") combat, with its requirement of faster reactions from players appealing to those who were more used to [action games](/wiki/Action_game "Action game").[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoguidiceBarton200982-169)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Nintendo-Super-Famicom-Set-FL.jpg/250px-Nintendo-Super-Famicom-Set-FL.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nintendo-Super-Famicom-Set-FL.jpg)

The [Super Famicom](/wiki/Super_Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Super Nintendo Entertainment System"), released on November 21, 1990

Nintendo executives were initially reluctant to design a new system, but as the market transitioned to the newer hardware, Nintendo saw the erosion of the commanding market share it had built up with the Nintendo Entertainment System.[[170]](#cite_note-170) Nintendo's fourth-generation console, the Super Famicom, was released in Japan on November 21, 1990; Nintendo's initial shipment of 300,000 units sold out within hours.[[171]](#cite_note-171) Despite stiff competition from the [Mega Drive/Genesis](/wiki/Sega_Genesis "Sega Genesis") console, the Super NES eventually took the top selling position, selling 49.10 million units worldwide,[[172]](#cite_note-consolidatedsales-172) and would remain popular well into the [fifth generation](/wiki/History_of_video_game_consoles_(fifth_generation) "History of video game consoles (fifth generation)") of consoles.[[173]](#cite_note-173) Nintendo's market position was defined by their machine's increased video and sound capabilities,[[174]](#cite_note-174) as well as exclusive first-party franchise titles such as *[Super Mario World](/wiki/Super_Mario_World "Super Mario World")*, *[The Legend of Zelda: A Link to the Past](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda:_A_Link_to_the_Past "The Legend of Zelda: A Link to the Past")* and *[Super Metroid](/wiki/Super_Metroid "Super Metroid")*.

In the early 1990s, the arcades experienced a major resurgence with the 1991 release of [Capcom](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom")'s *[Street Fighter II](/wiki/Street_Fighter_II "Street Fighter II")*,[[175]](#cite_note-175) which popularized competitive [fighting games](/wiki/Fighting_game "Fighting game") and revived the arcade industry to a level of popularity not seen since the days of *Pac-Man*,[[176]](#cite_note-tao2-176) setting off a renaissance for the arcade game industry in the early 1990s.[[177]](#cite_note-177) Its success led to a wave of other popular games which mostly were in the fighting genre, such as *[Fatal Fury: King of Fighters](/wiki/Fatal_Fury:_King_of_Fighters "Fatal Fury: King of Fighters")* (1992) by [SNK](/wiki/SNK_Playmore "SNK Playmore"), *[Virtua Fighter](/wiki/Virtua_Fighter "Virtua Fighter")* (1993) by [SEGA](/wiki/SEGA "SEGA"), and *[The King of Fighters](/wiki/The_King_of_Fighters "The King of Fighters")* (1994–2005) by SNK. In 1993, *[Electronic Games](/wiki/Electronic_Games "Electronic Games")* noted that when "historians look back at the world of coin-op during the early 1990s, one of the defining highlights of the video game art form will undoubtedly focus on fighting/martial arts themes" which it described as "the backbone of the industry" at the time.[[178]](#cite_note-178)

A new type of shoot 'em up emerged in the early 1990s: variously termed "bullet hell", "manic shooters", "maniac shooters" and *danmaku* (弾幕; "barrage"), these games required the player to dodge overwhelming numbers of enemy projectiles and called for still more consistent reactions from players.[[54]](#cite_note-lecture-54)[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAshcraft200877-179) Bullet hell games arose from the need for 2D shoot 'em up developers to compete with the emerging popularity of 3D games: huge numbers of missiles on screen were intended to impress players.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAshcraft200877-179) [Toaplan](/wiki/Toaplan "Toaplan")'s *[Batsugun](/wiki/Batsugun "Batsugun")* (1993) provided the prototypical template for this new breed,[[180]](#cite_note-180)[[181]](#cite_note-181) with [Cave](/wiki/Cave_(company) "Cave (company)") (formed by former employees of Toaplan, including *Batsugun's* main creator Tsuneki Ikeda, after the latter company collapsed) inventing the type proper with 1995's *[DonPachi](/wiki/DonPachi "DonPachi")*.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAshcraft200878–80-182) Bullet hell games marked another point where the shoot 'em up genre began to cater to more dedicated players.[[54]](#cite_note-lecture-54)[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAshcraft200877-179) Games such as *Gradius* had been more difficult than *Space Invaders* or *Xevious*,[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAshcraft200876-109) but bullet hell games were yet more inward-looking and aimed at dedicated fans of the genre looking for greater challenges.[[54]](#cite_note-lecture-54)[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAshcraft200877–78-183) While shooter games featuring protagonists on foot largely moved to 3D-based genres, popular, long-running series such as *[Contra](/wiki/Contra_(series) "Contra (series)")* and *[Metal Slug](/wiki/Metal_Slug_(series) "Metal Slug (series)")* continued to receive new sequels.[[184]](#cite_note-contraDS-184)[[185]](#cite_note-185)[[186]](#cite_note-186) Rail shooters have rarely been released in the new millennium, with only *[Rez](/wiki/Rez_(video_game) "Rez (video game)")* and *[Panzer Dragoon Orta](/wiki/Panzer_Dragoon_Orta "Panzer Dragoon Orta")* achieving cult recognition.[[187]](#cite_note-panzer-187)[[108]](#cite_note-1upharrier-108)[[188]](#cite_note-188)

1992 saw the release of *[Dragon Quest V](/wiki/Dragon_Quest_V "Dragon Quest V")*, a game that has been praised for its involving, emotional family-themed narrative divided by different periods of time, something that has appeared in very few video games before or since.[[189]](#cite_note-gamasutra_dq-189)[[190]](#cite_note-hg_dqv-190) It has also been credited as the first known video game to feature a playable pregnancy, a concept that has since appeared in later games such as *[Story of Seasons](/wiki/Story_of_Seasons_(series) "Story of Seasons (series)")*.[[191]](#cite_note-191) *Dragon Quest V's* monster-collecting mechanic, where monsters can be defeated, captured, added to the party, and gain their own experience levels, also influenced many later franchises such as *[Pokémon](/wiki/Pok%C3%A9mon "Pokémon")*, *[Digimon](/wiki/Digimon "Digimon")* and *[Dokapon](/wiki/Dokapon "Dokapon")*. In turn, the concept of collecting everything in a game, in the form of [achievements](/wiki/Achievement_(video_gaming) "Achievement (video gaming)") or similar rewards, has since become a common trend in video games.[[192]](#cite_note-192) *[Shin Megami Tensei](/wiki/Shin_Megami_Tensei "Shin Megami Tensei")*, released in 1992 for the SNES, introduced an early moral [alignment](/wiki/Alignment_(role-playing_games) "Alignment (role-playing games)") system that influences the direction and outcome of the storyline, leading to different possible paths and [multiple endings](/wiki/Multiple_endings "Multiple endings"). This has since become a hallmark of the *[Megami Tensei](/wiki/Megami_Tensei "Megami Tensei")* series.[[137]](#cite_note-HardcoreMegaten-137) Another non-linear RPG released that year was *[Romancing Saga](/wiki/Romancing_Saga "Romancing Saga")*, an open-world RPG by Square that offered many choices and allowed players to complete [quests](/wiki/Quest_(gaming) "Quest (gaming)") in any order, with the decision of whether or not to participate in any particular quest affecting the outcome of the storyline. The game also allowed players to choose from eight different characters, each with their own stories that start in different places and offer different outcomes.[[193]](#cite_note-ign_romancing_saga-193) [Data East](/wiki/Data_East "Data East")'s *[Heracles no Eikō III](/wiki/Glory_of_Heracles_(series) "Glory of Heracles (series)")*, written by [Kazushige Nojima](/wiki/Kazushige_Nojima "Kazushige Nojima"), introduced the plot element of a nameless [immortal](/wiki/Immortality "Immortality") suffering from [amnesia](/wiki/Amnesia "Amnesia"), and Nojima would later revisit the amnesia theme in *Final Fantasy VII* and *[Glory of Heracles](/wiki/Glory_of_Heracles "Glory of Heracles")*.[[194]](#cite_note-194) The [TurboGrafx-CD](/wiki/TurboGrafx-CD "TurboGrafx-CD") port of *[Dragon Knight II](/wiki/Dragon_Knight_II "Dragon Knight II")* released that year was also notable for introducing [erotic](/wiki/Eroge "Eroge") [adult](/wiki/Adult_video_game "Adult video game") content to consoles,[[195]](#cite_note-195)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] though such content had often appeared in Japanese computer RPGs since the early 1980s.[[126]](#cite_note-retro_3-126) That same year, [Game Arts](/wiki/Game_Arts "Game Arts") began the *[Lunar](/wiki/Lunar_(series) "Lunar (series)")* series on the [Sega CD](/wiki/Sega_CD "Sega CD") with *[Lunar: The Silver Star](/wiki/Lunar:_The_Silver_Star "Lunar: The Silver Star")*, one of the first successful CD-ROM RPGs, featuring both voice and text, and considered one of the best RPGs in its time.[[196]](#cite_note-gamefan_lunar-196) The game was praised for its soundtrack, emotionally engaging storyline, and strong characterization.[[197]](#cite_note-197) It also introduced an early form of level-scaling where the bosses would get stronger depending on the protagonist's level,[[198]](#cite_note-198) a mechanic that was later used in Enix's *[The 7th Saga](/wiki/The_7th_Saga "The 7th Saga")*[[199]](#cite_note-199) and extended to normal enemies in Square's *Romancing Saga 3* and later *Final Fantasy VIII*.[[200]](#cite_note-rpgfan_romancingsaga3-200)

[3D polygon graphics](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics") were popularized by the [Sega Model 1](/wiki/Sega_Model_1 "Sega Model 1") games *[Virtua Racing](/wiki/Virtua_Racing "Virtua Racing")* (1992) and *[Virtua Fighter](/wiki/Virtua_Fighter_(arcade_game) "Virtua Fighter (arcade game)")* (1993),[[201]](#cite_note-vracing-201) followed by [racing games](/wiki/Racing_video_game "Racing video game")[[176]](#cite_note-tao2-176) like the [Namco System 22](/wiki/Namco_System_22 "Namco System 22") title *[Ridge Racer](/wiki/Ridge_Racer "Ridge Racer")* (1993) and [Sega Model 2](/wiki/Sega_Model_2 "Sega Model 2") title *[Daytona USA](/wiki/Daytona_USA_(video_game) "Daytona USA (video game)")*, and [light gun shooters](/wiki/Light_gun_shooter "Light gun shooter") like Sega's *[Virtua Cop](/wiki/Virtua_Cop "Virtua Cop")* (1994),[[202]](#cite_note-ngage-202) gaining considerable popularity in the arcades.[[176]](#cite_note-tao2-176)

In 1993, Square's *[Secret of Mana](/wiki/Secret_of_Mana "Secret of Mana")*, the second in the *Mana* series, further advanced the [action RPG](/wiki/Action_role-playing_game "Action role-playing game") subgenre with its introduction of [cooperative multiplayer](/wiki/Cooperative_video_game "Cooperative video game") into the genre. The game was created by a team previously responsible for the first three *Final Fantasy* titles: [Nasir Gebelli](/wiki/Nasir_Gebelli "Nasir Gebelli"), [Koichi Ishii](/wiki/Koichi_Ishii "Koichi Ishii"), and [Hiromichi Tanaka](/wiki/Hiromichi_Tanaka "Hiromichi Tanaka").[[203]](#cite_note-1up_seiken-203) The game received considerable acclaim,[[204]](#cite_note-eurog_mana-204) for its innovative [pausable real-time](/wiki/Time-keeping_systems_in_games#Pausable_real-time "Time-keeping systems in games") battle system,[[205]](#cite_note-205)[[206]](#cite_note-apple_mana-206) the "[Ring Command](/wiki/Radial_menu "Radial menu")" menu system,[[206]](#cite_note-apple_mana-206) its innovative cooperative [multiplayer](/wiki/Multiplayer "Multiplayer") gameplay,[[204]](#cite_note-eurog_mana-204) where the second or third players could drop in and out of the game at any time rather than players having to join the game at the same time,[[207]](#cite_note-nowgamer_dungeon_siege_iii-207) and the customizable AI settings for computer-controlled allies.[[208]](#cite_note-208) The game has influenced a number of later action RPGs.[[207]](#cite_note-nowgamer_dungeon_siege_iii-207)[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarton2008220-209) That same year also saw the release of *[Phantasy Star IV: The End of the Millennium](/wiki/Phantasy_Star_IV:_The_End_of_the_Millennium "Phantasy Star IV: The End of the Millennium")*, which introduced the use of pre-programmable combat manoeuvers called 'macros', a means of setting up the player's party AI to deliver custom attack combos.[[138]](#cite_note-cvg_phantasy-138) That year also saw the release of *[Romancing Saga 2](/wiki/Romancing_Saga_2 "Romancing Saga 2")*, which further expanded the non-linear gameplay of its predecessor. While in the original *Romancing Saga*, scenarios were changed according to dialogue choices during conversations, *Romancing Saga 2* further expanded on this by having unique storylines for each character that can change depending on the player's actions, including who is chosen, what is said in conversation, what events have occurred, and who is present in the party.[[210]](#cite_note-210) *[PCGamesN](/wiki/PCGamesN "PCGamesN")* credits *Romancing SaGa 2* for having laid the foundations for modern Japanese RPGs with its progressive, non-linear, [open world](/wiki/Open_world "Open world") design and subversive themes.[[211]](#cite_note-211)

In 1994, *[Final Fantasy VI](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_VI "Final Fantasy VI")* moved away from the [medieval](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") setting of its predecessors, instead being set in a [steampunk](/wiki/Steampunk "Steampunk") environment,.[[212]](#cite_note-NPC_in_Jidor-212) The game received considerable acclaim, and is seen as one of the greatest RPGs of all time,[[213]](#cite_note-213) for improvements such as its broadened thematic scope,[[214]](#cite_note-nintendopower2-214) plotlines, characters, multiple-choice scenarios,[[215]](#cite_note-Scary1994-215) and variation of play.[[216]](#cite_note-nintendopower3-216) *Final Fantasy VI* dealt with mature themes such as [suicide](/wiki/Suicide "Suicide"), [war crimes](/wiki/War_crimes "War crimes"), [child abandonment](/wiki/Child_abandonment "Child abandonment"), [teen pregnancy](/wiki/Teen_pregnancy "Teen pregnancy"), and coping with the deaths of loved
