ones.[[217]](#cite_note-217) Square's *[Live A Live](/wiki/Live_A_Live "Live A Live")*, released for the Super Famicom in Japan, featured eight different characters and stories, with the first seven unfolding in any order the player chooses, as well as four different endings.[[218]](#cite_note-gtell_bestsnes-218) The game's [ninja](/wiki/Ninja "Ninja") chapter in particular was an early example of [stealth game](/wiki/Stealth_game "Stealth game") elements in an RPG, requiring the player to infiltrate a castle, rewarding the player if the entire chapter can be completed without engaging in combat.[[218]](#cite_note-gtell_bestsnes-218) Other chapters had similar innovations, such as Akira's chapter where the character uses telepathic powers to discover information.[[218]](#cite_note-gtell_bestsnes-218) *[Robotrek](/wiki/Robotrek "Robotrek")* by [Quintet](/wiki/Quintet_(developer) "Quintet (developer)") and [Ancient](/wiki/Ancient_(company) "Ancient (company)") was a predecessor to *Pokémon* in the sense that the protagonist does not himself fight, but sends out his robots to do so. Like *Pokémon*, *Robotrek* was designed to appeal to a younger audience, allowed team customization, and each robot was kept in a ball.[[219]](#cite_note-nextgen_kaiser-219)

[FromSoftware](/wiki/FromSoftware "FromSoftware") released their first video game, titled *[King's Field](/wiki/King%27s_Field_(video_game) "King's Field (video game)")*, as a launch title for the [PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation "PlayStation") in 1994.[[220]](#cite_note-220) The game was later called the brainchild of company founder Naotoshi Zin, who was considered a key creative figure in the series.[[221]](#cite_note-RollingMiyazaki-221) The eventual success of the first *King's Field* prompted the development of sequels, establishing the *[King's Field](/wiki/King%27s_Field "King's Field")* series.[[222]](#cite_note-IGNhistory-222)[[223]](#cite_note-KingsInterview-223) The design of *King's Field* would influence later titles by FromSoftware including *[Shadow Tower](/wiki/Shadow_Tower "Shadow Tower")*, *[Demon's Souls](/wiki/Demon%27s_Souls "Demon's Souls")*, and the *[Dark Souls](/wiki/Dark_Souls "Dark Souls")* series. The latter would propel FromSoftware to international fame.[[222]](#cite_note-IGNhistory-222)[[221]](#cite_note-RollingMiyazaki-221)[[224]](#cite_note-224)

In 1995, Square's *[Romancing Saga 3](/wiki/Romancing_Saga_3 "Romancing Saga 3")* featured a storyline that could be told differently from the perspectives of up to eight different characters and introduced a level-scaling system where the enemies get stronger as the characters do,[[200]](#cite_note-rpgfan_romancingsaga3-200) a mechanic that was later used in a number of later RPGs, including *[Final Fantasy VIII](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_VIII "Final Fantasy VIII")*.[[225]](#cite_note-rpgamer_ffviii-225) Sega's *[Sakura Wars](/wiki/Sakura_Wars "Sakura Wars")* for the [Saturn](/wiki/Sega_Saturn "Sega Saturn") combined tactical RPG combat with [dating sim](/wiki/Dating_sim "Dating sim") and [visual novel](/wiki/Visual_novel "Visual novel") elements, introducing a [real-time](/wiki/Real-time_game "Real-time game") [branching choice](/wiki/Nonlinear_gameplay#Branching_storylines "Nonlinear gameplay") system where, during an event or conversation, the player must choose an action or [dialogue choice](/wiki/Dialog_tree "Dialog tree") within a time limit, or not to respond at all within that time; the player's choice, or lack thereof, affects the [player character](/wiki/Player_character "Player character")'s relationship with other characters and in turn the characters' performance in battle, the direction of the storyline, and the ending. Later games in the series added several variations, including an action gauge that can be raised up or down depending on the situation, and a gauge that the player can manipulate using the [analog stick](/wiki/Analog_stick "Analog stick") depending on the situation.[[226]](#cite_note-226) The success of *Sakura Wars* led to a wave of games that combine the RPG and dating sim genres, including *[Thousand Arms](/wiki/Thousand_Arms "Thousand Arms")* in 1998, *[Riviera: The Promised Land](/wiki/Riviera:_The_Promised_Land "Riviera: The Promised Land")* in 2002, and *[Luminous Arc](/wiki/Luminous_Arc_(video_game) "Luminous Arc (video game)")* in 2007.[[227]](#cite_note-227)

The [survival horror](/wiki/Survival_horror "Survival horror") [video game genre](/wiki/Video_game_genre "Video game genre") began with [Capcom](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom")'s *[Resident Evil](/wiki/Resident_Evil_(1996_video_game) "Resident Evil (1996 video game)")* (1996), which coined the term "survival horror" and defined the genre.[[228]](#cite_note-GSREH-228)[[229]](#cite_note-229) The game was inspired by Capcom's *[Sweet Home](/wiki/Sweet_Home_(video_game) "Sweet Home (video game)")* (1989), retroactively described as survival horror.[[230]](#cite_note-UGOsweethome-230)

The first [Tokyo Game Show](/wiki/Tokyo_Game_Show "Tokyo Game Show") was held in [1996](/wiki/1996_in_video_gaming "1996 in video gaming").[[231]](#cite_note-231) From 1996 to 2002, the show was held twice a year: once in the Spring and once in Autumn (in the Tokyo Big Sight).[[232]](#cite_note-232) Since 2002, the show has been held once a year. It attracts more visitors every year. 2011's show hosted over 200,000 attendees and the 2012 show bringing in 223,753. The busiest TGS was in 2016 with 271,224 people in attendance and 614 companies had exhibits.[[233]](#cite_note-233) The event has been held annually since 1996 and was never canceled. The 20th anniversary of TGS was celebrated in 2016.[[234]](#cite_note-234)

The [Fujitsu](/wiki/Fujitsu "Fujitsu") [FM Towns Marty](/wiki/FM_Towns_Marty "FM Towns Marty") is considered the world's first 32-bit console (predating the [Amiga CD32](/wiki/Amiga_CD32 "Amiga CD32") and [3DO](/wiki/3DO_Interactive_Multiplayer "3DO Interactive Multiplayer")), being only released in Japan on February 20, 1993, by [Fujitsu](/wiki/Fujitsu "Fujitsu"). However, it failed to make an impact in the marketplace due to its expense relative to other consoles and inability to compete with home computers.[[235]](#cite_note-235)[[236]](#cite_note-236) Around the mid-1990s, the [fifth-generation](/wiki/Fifth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Fifth generation of video game consoles") home consoles, [Sega Saturn](/wiki/Sega_Saturn "Sega Saturn"), [PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation_(console) "PlayStation (console)"), and [Nintendo 64](/wiki/Nintendo_64 "Nintendo 64"), began offering [true 3D](/wiki/True_3D "True 3D") graphics, improved sound, and better 2D graphics, than the previous generation. By 1995, personal computers followed, with [3D accelerator](/wiki/3D_accelerator "3D accelerator") cards. While [arcade systems](/wiki/Arcade_system_board "Arcade system board") such as the [Sega Model 3](/wiki/Sega_Model_3 "Sega Model 3") remained considerably more advanced than home systems in the late 1990s.[[237]](#cite_note-237)[[238]](#cite_note-238)

The next major revolution came in the mid-to-late 1990s, which saw the rise of [3D computer graphics](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics") and [optical discs](/wiki/Optical_disc "Optical disc") in [fifth generation](/wiki/Fifth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Fifth generation of video game consoles") consoles. The implications for RPGs were enormous—longer, more involved quests, better audio, and [full-motion video](/wiki/Full-motion_video "Full-motion video"). This was clearly demonstrated in 1997 by the phenomenal success of *[Final Fantasy VII](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_VII "Final Fantasy VII")*, which is considered one of the most influential games of all time,[[239]](#cite_note-1up_ffvii-239)[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoguidiceBarton200984-240) akin to that of *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* in the movie industry. With a record-breaking production budget of around $45 million,[[239]](#cite_note-1up_ffvii-239) the ambitious scope of *Final Fantasy VII* raised the possibilities for the genre, with its more expansive world to explore,[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoguidiceBarton200977-241) much longer quest, more numerous sidequests,[[239]](#cite_note-1up_ffvii-239) [dozens of minigames](/wiki/Minigames_of_Final_Fantasy "Minigames of Final Fantasy"), and much higher production values. The latter includes innovations such as the use of 3D characters on pre-rendered backgrounds,[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoguidiceBarton200978-242) battles viewed from multiple different angles rather than a single angle, and for the first time full-motion [CGI](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery") video seamlessly blended into the gameplay,[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoguidiceBarton200984-240) effectively integrated throughout the game.[[239]](#cite_note-1up_ffvii-239) Gameplay innovations included the materia system, which allowed a considerable amount of customization and flexibility through materia that can be combined in many different ways and exchanged between characters at any time, and the limit breaks, special attacks that can be performed after a character's limit meter fills up by taking hits from opponents.[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoguidiceBarton200984-240) *Final Fantasy VII* continues to be listed among the [best games of all time](/wiki/List_of_video_games_considered_the_best "List of video games considered the best"), for its highly polished gameplay, high playability, lavish production, well-developed characters, intricate storyline,[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoguidiceBarton200978-242) and an emotionally engaging narrative that is much darker and sophisticated than most other RPGs.[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoguidiceBarton200986-243) The game's storytelling and character development was considered a major narrative jump forward for video games and was often compared to films and novels at the time.[[244]](#cite_note-244)

One of the earliest Japanese RPGs, [Koei](/wiki/Koei "Koei")'s *[The Dragon and Princess](/wiki/History_of_Eastern_role-playing_video_games#Japanese_computer_role-playing_games "History of Eastern role-playing video games")* (1982),[[245]](#cite_note-oh_fm7-245) featured a [tactical turn-based](/wiki/Turn-based_tactics "Turn-based tactics") combat system.[[246]](#cite_note-dragon-princess-246)[[247]](#cite_note-247) Koji Sumii's *[Bokosuka Wars](/wiki/Bokosuka_Wars "Bokosuka Wars")*, originally released for the [Sharp X1](/wiki/Sharp_X1 "Sharp X1") computer in 1983[[248]](#cite_note-gspot_bokosuka-248) and later ported to the NES in 1985,[[249]](#cite_note-nintendo_bokosuka-249) is credited for laying the foundations for the [tactical RPG](/wiki/Tactical_RPG "Tactical RPG") genre, or "simulation RPG" genre as it is known in Japan, with its blend of basic RPG and [strategy game](/wiki/Strategy_game "Strategy game") elements.[[250]](#cite_note-Nintendo-Bokosuka-250) The genre became with the game that set the template for tactical RPGs, *[Fire Emblem: Ankoku Ryū to Hikari no Tsurugi](/wiki/Fire_Emblem:_Ankoku_Ry%C5%AB_to_Hikari_no_Tsurugi "Fire Emblem: Ankoku Ryū to Hikari no Tsurugi")* (1990).[[251]](#cite_note-Fire-Emblem-251)

Treasure's shoot 'em up, *[Radiant Silvergun](/wiki/Radiant_Silvergun "Radiant Silvergun")* (1998), introduced an element of narrative to the genre. It was critically acclaimed for its refined design, though it was not released outside Japan and remains a much sought after collector's item.[[130]](#cite_note-igntop10-130)[[54]](#cite_note-lecture-54)[[252]](#cite_note-252)[[253]](#cite_note-253) Its successor *[Ikaruga](/wiki/Ikaruga "Ikaruga")* (2001) featured improved graphics and was again acclaimed as one of the best games in the genre. Both *Radiant Silvergun* and *Ikaruga* were later released on [Xbox Live Arcade](/wiki/Xbox_Live_Arcade "Xbox Live Arcade").[[130]](#cite_note-igntop10-130)[[54]](#cite_note-lecture-54)[[254]](#cite_note-2dspace-254) The *[Touhou Project](/wiki/Touhou_Project "Touhou Project")* series spans 22 years and 27 games as of 2018 and was listed in the [Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records") in October 2010 for being the "most prolific fan-made shooter series".[[255]](#cite_note-255) The genre has undergone something of a resurgence with the release of the [Xbox 360](/wiki/Xbox_360 "Xbox 360"), [PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3 "PlayStation 3") and [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii") online services,[[254]](#cite_note-2dspace-254) while in Japan arcade shoot 'em ups retain a deep-rooted niche popularity.[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAshcraft200888-256) *[Geometry Wars: Retro Evolved](/wiki/Geometry_Wars:_Retro_Evolved "Geometry Wars: Retro Evolved")* was released on Xbox Live Arcade in 2005 and in particular stood out from the various re-releases and [casual games](/wiki/Casual_game "Casual game") available on the service.[[257]](#cite_note-257) The PC has also seen its share of [dōjin](/wiki/D%C5%8Djin_soft "Dōjin soft") shoot 'em ups like *[Crimzon Clover](/wiki/Crimzon_Clover "Crimzon Clover")*, *[Jamestown: Legend of the Lost Colony](/wiki/Jamestown:_Legend_of_the_Lost_Colony "Jamestown: Legend of the Lost Colony")*, *Xenoslaive Overdrive*, and the [*eXceed* series](/wiki/EXceed_(series) "EXceed (series)"). However, despite the genre's continued appeal to an enthusiastic niche of players, shoot 'em up developers are increasingly embattled financially by the power of home consoles and their attendant genres.[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAshcraft200888-256)[[258]](#cite_note-258)

### 2005–2015

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 2005–2015")]

See also: [Handheld game console](/wiki/Handheld_game_console "Handheld game console"), [Japanese mobile phone culture](/wiki/Japanese_mobile_phone_culture "Japanese mobile phone culture"), and [Seventh generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Seventh_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Seventh generation of video game consoles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/GitarFreaks_and_DrumMania_V_cabinets_and_player.jpg/250px-GitarFreaks_and_DrumMania_V_cabinets_and_player.jpg)](/wiki/File:GitarFreaks_and_DrumMania_V_cabinets_and_player.jpg)

A man playing a drumming [arcade game](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") (*[Drummania](/wiki/Drummania "Drummania")*) in [Tsukuba, Ibaraki](/wiki/Tsukuba,_Ibaraki "Tsukuba, Ibaraki"), 2005

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Girls_playing_video_games_in_Japan.jpg/250px-Girls_playing_video_games_in_Japan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Girls_playing_video_games_in_Japan.jpg)

[Girls](/wiki/Women_and_video_games "Women and video games") playing *[The House of the Dead III](/wiki/The_House_of_the_Dead_III "The House of the Dead III")* in an [amusement arcade](/wiki/Amusement_arcade "Amusement arcade") in Japan, 2005

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Tokyo_Game_Show_2006_-_PS3.jpg/250px-Tokyo_Game_Show_2006_-_PS3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_Game_Show_2006_-_PS3.jpg)

[PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3 "PlayStation 3") at [Tokyo Game Show](/wiki/Tokyo_Game_Show "Tokyo Game Show") 2006

In 2002, the Japanese video game industry made up about 50% of the global market; that share has since shrunk to around 10% by 2010.[[259]](#cite_note-Cieslak-259) The shrinkage in market share has been attributed to a difference of taste between Japanese and Western audiences,[[259]](#cite_note-Cieslak-259) and the country's [economic recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession").[[260]](#cite_note-capcom_market1-260)

Nintendo had seen record revenues, net sales and profits in 2009 as a result of the release of the [Nintendo DS](/wiki/Nintendo_DS "Nintendo DS") and [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii") in 2004 and 2006, respectively,[[261]](#cite_note-261)[[262]](#cite_note-262)[[263]](#cite_note-2009_Annual_Financial-263) but in Nintendo's subsequent years, its revenues had declined.[[264]](#cite_note-264)[[265]](#cite_note-265)

In 2009, [FromSoftware](/wiki/FromSoftware "FromSoftware") released *[Demon's Souls](/wiki/Demon%27s_Souls "Demon's Souls")* for the [PlayStation 3](/wiki/PlayStation_3 "PlayStation 3"), which brought them international exposure. Its [spiritual successor](/wiki/Spiritual_successor "Spiritual successor"), *[Dark Souls](/wiki/Dark_Souls_(video_game) "Dark Souls (video game)")*, was released in 2011. In March 2014, *[Dark Souls II](/wiki/Dark_Souls_II "Dark Souls II")*, was released, while *[Dark Souls III](/wiki/Dark_Souls_III "Dark Souls III")* was released in 2016.[[266]](#cite_note-266) Other FromSoftware games inspired by the *[Dark Souls](/wiki/Dark_Souls "Dark Souls")* series, *[Bloodborne](/wiki/Bloodborne "Bloodborne")* (2015) and *[Elden Ring](/wiki/Elden_Ring "Elden Ring")* (2022), received critical acclaim and strong sales.[[267]](#cite_note-267)[[268]](#cite_note-268) They have also received a number of awards, primarily those for the [role-playing](/wiki/Role-playing_video_game "Role-playing video game") genre, including multiple "RPG of the Year" and [Game of the Year](/wiki/List_of_Game_of_the_Year_awards "List of Game of the Year awards") awards.[[269]](#cite_note-269)[[270]](#cite_note-270)[[271]](#cite_note-271)[[272]](#cite_note-272) Since release, *Dark Souls* and *Bloodborne* have been cited by many publications to be among the [greatest games of all time](/wiki/Greatest_games_of_all_time "Greatest games of all time").[[273]](#cite_note-273)[[274]](#cite_note-274)[[275]](#cite_note-275)

The decline of the Japanese video game development industry during this period was partially attributed to the traditional development process. Japanese companies were criticized for long development times and slow release dates on home [video game consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console"), their lack of third-party [game engines](/wiki/Game_engine "Game engine"), and for being too insular to appeal to a global market.[[276]](#cite_note-276) [Yoichi Wada](/wiki/Yoichi_Wada "Yoichi Wada") stated in 2009 that the Japanese game industry had become "almost xenophobic."[[277]](#cite_note-Squenix_acquisitions-277) He also stated: "The lag with the US is very clear. The [US games industry](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_the_United_States "Video gaming in the United States") was not good in the past but it has now attracted people from the [computer industry](/wiki/PC_game "PC game") and from [Hollywood](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States"), which has led to strong growth."[[277]](#cite_note-Squenix_acquisitions-277)[[278]](#cite_note-278) At the 2010 [Tokyo Game Show](/wiki/Tokyo_Game_Show "Tokyo Game Show"), [Keiji Inafune](/wiki/Keiji_Inafune "Keiji Inafune") stated that "Everyone's making awful games - Japan is at least five years behind", and that "Japan is isolated in the gaming world. If something doesn't change, we're doomed.", stressing the need for Japanese developers to bring in Western approaches to game development to make a comeback.[[259]](#cite_note-Cieslak-259)

Related to the isolationism, games developed in Western countries did not perform well in Japan, whereas Japanese games were readily played by Western market consumers.[[279]](#cite_note-279)[[280]](#cite_note-280)[[281]](#cite_note-281)[[282]](#cite_note-282) Foreign games often sell more poorly in Japanese markets due to differences in what consumers expect for [escapism](/wiki/Escapism "Escapism") between these cultures.[[283]](#cite_note-283) Microsoft had attempted to push both the [Xbox](/wiki/Xbox_(console) "Xbox (console)") and [Xbox 360](/wiki/Xbox_360 "Xbox 360") consoles in Japan with poor success, at they struggled to compete against Sony and Nintendo there.[[284]](#cite_note-284)

However, as detailed above, Japanese console games became less successful, even in their own country, as of 2013.[[285]](#cite_note-285)[[286]](#cite_note-286)[[287]](#cite_note-287)

In the Japanese gaming industry, arcades have remained popular through to the present day. As of 2009, out of Japan's $20 billion gaming market, $6 billion of that amount is generated from arcades, which represent the largest sector of the Japanese video game market, followed by home [console games](/wiki/Console_game "Console game") and [mobile games](/wiki/Mobile_game "Mobile game") at $3.5 billion and $2 billion, respectively.[[288]](#cite_note-288) In 2005, arcade ownership and operation accounted for a majority of [Namco](/wiki/Namco "Namco")'s for example.[[289]](#cite_note-289) With considerable withdrawal from the arcade market from companies such as [Capcom](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom"), [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega") became the strongest player in the arcade market with 60% marketshare in 2006.[[290]](#cite_note-290) Despite the global decline of arcades, Japanese companies hit record revenue for three consecutive years during this period.[[291]](#cite_note-291) However, due to the country's [economic recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), the Japanese arcade industry has also been steadily declining, from [¥](/wiki/Japanese_yen "Japanese yen")702.9 billion (US$8.7 billion) in 2007 to ¥504.3 billion ($6.2 billion) in 2010.[[260]](#cite_note-capcom_market1-260) In 2013, estimation of revenue is ¥470 billion.[[260]](#cite_note-capcom_market1-260)

In the 2010s, Japanese RPGs have been experiencing a resurgence on PC, with a significant increase in the number of Japanese RPGs releasing for the [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)") platform. This began with the 2010 release of doujin/indie game *[Recettear](/wiki/Recettear "Recettear")* (2007) for Steam,[[292]](#cite_note-pcgamer-japan-292) selling over 500,000 units on the platform.[[293]](#cite_note-293) This led to many Japanese doujin/indie games releasing on Steam in subsequent years.[[292]](#cite_note-pcgamer-japan-292)

Beyond doujin/indie titles, 2012 was a breakthrough year, with the debut of [Nihon Falcom](/wiki/Nihon_Falcom "Nihon Falcom")'s *[Ys](/wiki/Ys_(series) "Ys (series)")* series on Steam and then the Steam release of [From Software](/wiki/From_Software "From Software")'s *[Dark Souls](/wiki/Dark_Souls "Dark Souls")*, which sold millions on the platform. Other Japanese RPGs were subsequently ported to Steam, such as the previously niche *[Valkyria Chronicles](/wiki/Valkyria_Chronicles "Valkyria Chronicles")* which became a million-seller on the platform, and other titles that sold hundreds of thousands on Steam, such as the 2014 localization of *[The Legend of Heroes: Trails in the Sky](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Heroes:_Trails_in_the_Sky "The Legend of Heroes: Trails in the Sky")* (2014) and ports of numerous *[Final Fantasy](/wiki/Final_Fantasy "Final Fantasy")* titles. Japanese developers have been increasingly considering Steam as a viable platform for the genre, with many Japanese RPGs available on the platform.[[292]](#cite_note-pcgamer-japan-292)

By 2015, Japan had become the world's fourth largest [PC game](/wiki/PC_game "PC game") market, behind only [China](/wiki/China "China"), the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea").[[294]](#cite_note-294) The Japanese game development engine [RPG Maker](/wiki/RPG_Maker "RPG Maker") has also gained significant popularity on Steam, including hundreds of commercial games. Every year, hundreds of games released on Steam are created using RPG Maker, as of 2017.[[295]](#cite_note-295)

In the present day, Japan is the world's largest market for [mobile games](/wiki/Mobile_game "Mobile game").[[296]](#cite_note-296) The Japanese market today is becoming increasingly dominated by mobile games, which generated $5.1 billion in 2013, more than traditional [console games](/wiki/Console_game "Console game") in the country.[[297]](#cite_note-297)

Former rivals in the Japanese arcade industry, [Konami](/wiki/Konami "Konami"), [Taito](/wiki/Taito "Taito"), [Bandai Namco Entertainment](/wiki/Bandai_Namco_Entertainment "Bandai Namco Entertainment") and [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega"), are now working together to keep the arcade industry vibrant.[[298]](#cite_note-298)

### 2016–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_Japan&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 2016–present")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Japanese_shmup_arcade.jpg/250px-Japanese_shmup_arcade.jpg)](/wiki/File:Japanese_shmup_arcade.jpg)

Japanese players at a [shoot 'em up](/wiki/Shoot_%27em_up "Shoot 'em up") arcade in [Akihabara, Tokyo](/wiki/Akihabara "Akihabara") (2017)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Switch%2C_Wii_U%2C_and_PS4_first-year_sales_in_Japan.png/250px-Switch%2C_Wii_U%2C_and_PS4_first-year_sales_in_Japan.png)](/wiki/File:Switch,_Wii_U,_and_PS4_first-year_sales_in_Japan.png)

A line graph depicting the first-year sales of the [Nintendo Switch](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch "Nintendo Switch") (red), [Wii U](/wiki/Wii_U "Wii U") (green), and [PlayStation 4](/wiki/PlayStation_4 "PlayStation 4") (blue) in Japan

The [eighth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Eighth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Eighth generation of video game consoles") primarily includes the [home video game consoles](/wiki/Home_video_game_console "Home video game console") of the [Wii U](/wiki/Wii_U "Wii U") released in 2012, the [PlayStation 4](/wiki/PlayStation_4 "PlayStation 4") family in 2013; the [handheld game consoles](/wiki/Handheld_game_console "Handheld game console") of the [Nintendo 3DS](/wiki/Nintendo_3DS "Nintendo 3DS") in 2011, [Nintendo 2DS](/wiki/Nintendo_2DS "Nintendo 2DS") in 2013, and the [PlayStation Vita](/wiki/PlayStation_Vita "PlayStation Vita") in 2011; as well as the first hybrid game console, the [Nintendo Switch](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch "Nintendo Switch") in 2017, which played as a handheld but could be [docked](/wiki/Docking_station "Docking station") to played like a home console. Unlike in most prior generations, there were few new innovative hardware capabilities to mark this generation as distinct from prior ones. Sony continued to produce new systems with similar designs and capabilities as their predecessors, but with improved performance (processing speed, higher-resolution graphics, and increased storage capacity) that further moved consoles into confluence with personal computers, and furthering support for [digital distribution](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution") and [games as a service](/wiki/Games_as_a_service "Games as a service"). [Motion-controlled games](/wiki/Motion_controller "Motion controller") of the seventh generation had waned in popularity, but consoles were preparing for advancement of [virtual reality](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality") (VR); Sony introduced the [PlayStation VR](/wiki/PlayStation_VR "PlayStation VR") in 2016.[[299]](#cite_note-299)[[300]](#cite_note-300)

Though prior console generations have normally occurred in five to six-year cycles, the transition from seventh to eighth generation lasted approximately eight years.[[301]](#cite_note-buzz-301) The transition is also unusual in that the prior generation's best-selling unit, the Wii, was the first to be replaced in the eighth generation.[[301]](#cite_note-buzz-301) In 2011, Sony considered themselves only halfway through a ten-year lifecycle for their seventh-generation offerings.[[302]](#cite_note-302)[[303]](#cite_note-303) Nintendo president [Satoru Iwata](/wiki/Satoru_Iwata "Satoru Iwata") had stated that his company would be releasing the Wii U due to declining sales of seventh generation home consoles and that "the market is now waiting for a new proposal for home consoles".[[304]](#cite_note-Iwata-304) Sony considered making its next console a digital download only machine, but decided against it due to concerns about the inconsistency of internet speeds available globally, especially in [developing countries](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country").[[305]](#cite_note-305)

On September 13, 2012, Nintendo announced that the Wii U would launch in Japan on December 8, 2012.[[306]](#cite_note-306) The PlayStation 4 and Wii U use [AMD](/wiki/AMD "AMD") [GPUs](/wiki/Graphics_processing_unit "Graphics processing unit"), and the PS4 also use AMD [CPUs](/wiki/Central_processing_unit "Central processing unit") on an [x86-64](/wiki/X86-64 "X86-64") architecture, similar to common [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") (as opposed to the IBM [PowerPC](/wiki/PowerPC "PowerPC") Architecture used in the previous generation). Nintendo and Sony were not aware that they were all using AMD hardware until their consoles were announced.[[307]](#cite_note-e12a-307) This shift was considered to be beneficial for [multi-platform](/wiki/Multi_platform "Multi platform") development, due to the increased similarities between PC hardware and console hardware.[[308]](#cite_note-v21a-308)

In October 2013, online retailer *Play.com* announced that its Wii U sales saw a 75% sales increase. The company also predicted that the Wii U would be more popular than its competition, the [PlayStation 4](/wiki/PlayStation_4 "PlayStation 4") and [Xbox One](/wiki/Xbox_One "Xbox One"), among children during the holiday season.[[309]](#cite_note-309) Following the release of *[Wii Party U](/wiki/Wii_Party_U "Wii Party U")* on October 31 in Japan, weekly Wii U sales spiked to 38,802 units sold.[[310]](#cite_note-310) During the first two weeks of December, the Wii U was the top performing home console in Japan, with 123,665 units sold.[[311]](#cite_note-311) In [fiscal year](/wiki/Fiscal_year "Fiscal year") (FY) 2013 (ending early 2013), Nintendo sold 23.7 million consoles.[[312]](#cite_note-consolidatedsales_2013-312) By February 26, 2014, Wii U sales had surpassed those of the [Xbox 360](/wiki/Xbox_360 "Xbox 360") in Japan.[[313]](#cite_note-313) However, by June 2015, the basic Wii U was discontinued in Japan, and replaced by a 32 GB "Premium" set that includes white hardware and a Wii Remote Plus.[[314]](#cite_note-314)[[315]](#cite_note-315) In mid-November 2016, Nintendo announced that Japanese production of the Wii U would be ending "in the near future".[[316]](#cite_note-316)

The PS4 was released in Japan at ¥39,980 on February 22, 2014.[[317]](#cite_note-317) In September 2015, Sony reduced the price of the PS4 in Japan to ¥34,980,[[318]](#cite_note-318) with similar price drops in other Southeast Asian markets.[[319]](#cite_note-319) Within the first two days of release in Japan during the weekend of February 22, 2014, 322,083 consoles were sold.[[320]](#cite_note-320) PS4 software unit sales surpassed 20.5 million on April 13, 2014.[[321]](#cite_note-7mil_gem-321) During Japan's 2013 [fiscal year](/wiki/Fiscal_year "Fiscal year"), heightened demand for the PS4 helped Sony top global console sales, beating Nintendo for the first time in eight years.[[322]](#cite_note-PS4sales-322)

Since 2016, Japanese video games have been experiencing a resurgence,[[323]](#cite_note-gamerevolution2016-323)[[324]](#cite_note-usgamer2016-324)[[325]](#cite_note-technobuffalo2016-325) as part of a renaissance for the Japanese video game industry.[[326]](#cite_note-verge2016-326)[[327]](#cite_note-327) In 2017, Japanese video games gained further commercial success and greater critical acclaim.[[323]](#cite_note-gamerevolution2016-323)[[326]](#cite_note-verge2016-326) In 2016, the global success of *[Pokémon Go](/wiki/Pok%C3%A9mon_Go "Pokémon Go")* helped *[Pokémon Sun and Moon](/wiki/Pok%C3%A9mon_Sun_and_Moon "Pokémon Sun and Moon")* set sales records around the world.[[324]](#cite_note-usgamer2016-324) *[Final Fantasy XV](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_XV "Final Fantasy XV")* was also a major success, selling millions. There were also other Japanese RPGs that earned commercial success and/or critical acclaim that year, including *[Dragon Quest VII: Fragments of the Forgotten Past](/wiki/Dragon_Quest_VII:_Fragments_of_the_Forgotten_Past "Dragon Quest VII: Fragments of the Forgotten Past")*, *[Shin Megami Tensei IV: Apocalypse](/wiki/Shin_Megami_Tensei_IV:_Apocalypse "Shin Megami Tensei IV: Apocalypse")*, *[Bravely Second](/wiki/Bravely_Second "Bravely Second")*, *[Fire Emblem Fates](/wiki/Fire_Emblem_Fates "Fire Emblem Fates")*, *[Dragon Quest Builders](/wiki/Dragon_Quest_Builders "Dragon Quest Builders")*, *[World of Final Fantasy](/wiki/World_of_Final_Fantasy "World of Final Fantasy")*, *[Exist Archive: The Other Side of the Sky](/wiki/Exist_Archive:_The_Other_Side_of_the_Sky "Exist Archive: The Other Side of the Sky")* and *[I Am Setsuna](/wiki/I_Am_Setsuna "I Am Setsuna")*.[[325]](#cite_note-technobuffalo2016-325)

Anticipating the release of the console's successor, the [Nintendo Switch](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch "Nintendo Switch"), a [hybrid video game console](/wiki/Hybrid_video_game_console "Hybrid video game console"), Nintendo had planned to diminish production of the Wii U. It formally announced the end of its production on January 31, 2017.[[328]](#cite_note-328)[[329]](#cite_note-kotaku_eol2-329) The company had posted its first loss as a video game company in 2012 prior to the [Wii U](/wiki/Wii_U "Wii U")'s introduction that year, and had similar losses in the following years due to the console's poor uptake.[[330]](#cite_note-330) *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* attributed Nintendo lowering financial forecasts in 2014 to weak hardware sales against [mobile gaming](/wiki/Mobile_gaming "Mobile gaming").[[331]](#cite_note-331) Previously, the company had been hesitant about this market, with then-president [Satoru Iwata](/wiki/Satoru_Iwata "Satoru Iwata") considering that they would "cease to be Nintendo" and lose their identity if they attempted to enter it.[[332]](#cite_note-332) About three years prior to the Switch's announcement, Iwata, [Tatsumi Kimishima](/wiki/Tatsumi_Kimishima "Tatsumi Kimishima"), [Genyo Takeda](/wiki/Genyo_Takeda "Genyo Takeda"), and [Shigeru Miyamoto](/wiki/Shigeru_Miyamoto "Shigeru Miyamoto") crafted a strategy for revitalizing Nintendo's business model, which included approaching the mobile market, creating new hardware, and "maximizing [their] intellectual property".[[333]](#cite_note-bloomberg-333) Prior to his death, Iwata was able to secure a business alliance with Japanese mobile provider [DeNA](/wiki/DeNA "DeNA") to develop mobile titles based on Nintendo's first-party franchises, believing this approach would not compromise their integrity.[[334]](#cite_note-334)[[335]](#cite_note-335) Following Iwata's death in July 2015, Kimishima was named as [president](/wiki/President_(corporate_title) "President (corporate title)") of Nintendo, while Miyamoto was promoted to the title of "Creative Fellow".[[333]](#cite_note-bloomberg-333)

The Switch was officially released on March 3, 2017, in Japan with an [MSRP](/wiki/MSRP "MSRP") of [¥](/wiki/Japanese_yen "Japanese yen")29,980.[[336]](#cite_note-cnbc-336) The design of the Switch was aimed to bridge the polarization of the gaming market at the time, creating a device that could play "leisurely" video games along with games that are aimed to be played "deeply", according to Shinya Takahashi and [Yoshiaki Koizumi](/wiki/Yoshiaki_Koizumi "Yoshiaki Koizumi"), general manager and deputy general manager of Nintendo's [Entertainment Planning & Development Division](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_Planning_%26_Development "Nintendo Entertainment Planning & Development") (EPD), respectively.[[337]](#cite_note-glixel_history-337) This approach also would apply to the cultural lifestyle and gaming differences between Japanese and Western players; Japanese players tend to play on the go and with social groups, while Western players tend to play at home by themselves.[[338]](#cite_note-time_feb2017-338) The design of the Switch would meet both cultures, and certain games, like *[1-2-Switch](/wiki/1-2-Switch "1-2-Switch")*, could potentially make social gaming more acceptable in Western culture.[[339]](#cite_note-time_kimishima-339) Two key elements that were set to address this mixed market were the ability for the unit to play both on a television screen and as a portable, and the use of detachable controllers.[[337]](#cite_note-glixel_history-337) In Japan, first weekend sales exceeded 330,000 units, which was on par with the [PlayStation 4](/wiki/PlayStation_4 "PlayStation 4") during its launch period.[[340]](#cite_note-JP-twoday-340)[[341]](#cite_note-JP-threeday-341) [Media Create](/wiki/Media_Create "Media Create") estimated that more than 500,000 Switch units were sold in Japan within its first month, beating out the PlayStation 4 to this figure.[[342]](#cite_note-342)

Console sales in Japan, which had been languishing due to the strength of the mobile game market, saw its first annual growth of 14.8% in 2017 due to the release of the Switch.[[343]](#cite_note-343) Based on its first year sales, the Switch was considered to be the fastest-selling game console in history in many regions. With 2017 year end Japanese sales data from Media Create, the Switch became the fastest-selling home console in Japan in first year sales, with its total sales of 3.2 million units exceeding the 3.0 million units of the [PlayStation 2](/wiki/PlayStation_2 "PlayStation 2") during its first year of release,[[344]](#cite_note-344)[[345]](#cite_note-345) while *[Famitsu](/wiki/Famitsu "Famitsu")* reported that these sales had eclipsed the lifetime sales of the Wii U in the country,[[346]](#cite_note-346) and helped to support the first growth in sales within Japan's console market in eleven years.[[347]](#cite_note-347) By May 2019, the Switch had overtaken the PS4's lifetime sales in Japan.[[348]](#cite_note-348)

In 2017, Japanese RPGs gained further commercial success and greater critical acclaim.[[323]](#cite_note-gamerevolution2016-323)[[326]](#cite_note-verge2016-326) The year started strong with *[Gravity Rush 2](/wiki/Gravity_Rush_2 "Gravity Rush 2")*,[[326]](#cite_note-verge2016-326) followed by *[Yakuza 0](/wiki/Yakuza_0 "Yakuza 0")*, which some critics consider the best in the *[Yakuza](/wiki/Yakuza_(series) "Yakuza (series)")* series, *[Nioh](/wiki/Nioh "Nioh")* which is considered to have one of the [eighth-generation](/wiki/Eighth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Eighth generation of video game consoles")'s best RPG combat systems, and then *[Nier Automata](/wiki/Nier_Automata "Nier Automata")* which has gameplay and storytelling thought to be some of the best in recent years.[[323]](#cite_note-gamerevolution2016-323) *[Persona 5](/wiki/Persona_5 "Persona 5")* won the Best Role Playing Game award at [The Game Awards 2017](/wiki/The_Game_Awards_2017 "The Game Awards 2017").[[349]](#cite_note-349) Some Japanese RPGs that were previously considered niche became mainstream million-sellers in 2017, including *Persona 5*, *Nier: Automata*,[[326]](#cite_note-verge2016-326) *Nioh*,[[350]](#cite_note-350) and *[Xenoblade Chronicles 2](/wiki/Xenoblade_Chronicles_2 "Xenoblade Chronicles 2")* on the [Nintendo Switch](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch "Nintendo Switch").[[351]](#cite_note-351) 2017 was considered a strong year for Japanese RPGs, with other notable releases including *[Dragon Quest VIII](/wiki/Dragon_Quest_VIII "Dragon Quest VIII")* on the [Nintendo 3DS](/wiki/Nintendo_3DS "Nintendo 3DS"), *[Tales of Berseria](/wiki/Tales_of_Berseria "Tales of Berseria")*, *[Valkyria Revolution](/wiki/Valkyria_Revolution "Valkyria Revolution")*, *[Ever Oasis](/wiki/Ever_Oasis "Ever Oasis")*, *[Final Fantasy XII: The Zodiac Age](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_XII:_The_Zodiac_Age "Final Fantasy XII: The Zodiac Age")*, *[Ys VIII](/wiki/Ys_VIII "Ys VIII")*, *[Etrian Odyssey V](/wiki/Etrian_Odyssey_V "Etrian Odyssey V")*, *[Dragon Quest Heroes II](/wiki/Dragon_Quest_Heroes_II "Dragon Quest Heroes II")*, *[The Legend of Heroes: Trails in the Sky the 3rd](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Heroes:_Trails_in_the_Sky_the_3rd "The Legend of Heroes: Trails in the Sky the 3rd")*, *[Fire Emblem Echoes: Shadows of Valentia](/wiki/Fire_Emblem_Echoes:_Shadows_of_Valentia "Fire Emblem Echoes: Shadows of Valentia")*, *[Final Fantasy XIV: Stormblood](/wiki/Final_Fantasy_XIV:_Stormblood "Final Fantasy XIV: Stormblood")*, and *[Tokyo Xanadu](/wiki/Tokyo_Xanadu "Tokyo Xanadu")*.[[352]](#cite_note-352) In 2018, *[Monster Hunter: World](/wiki/Monster_Hunter:_World "Monster Hunter: World")* sold over 10 million units,[[353]](#cite_note-353) becoming Capcom's best-selling single software title,[[354]](#cite_note-354) and [Square Enix](/wiki/Square_Enix "Square Enix")'s *[Octopath Traveler](/wiki/Octopath_Traveler "Octopath Traveler")* sold over 1 million units.[[355]](#cite_note-355) In 2019, [From Software](/wiki/From_Software "From Software")'s [Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice](/wiki/Sekiro:_Shadows_Die_Twice "Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice") received universal acclaim. On release day, *Sekiro* drew over 108,000 concurrent players on [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)"), the highest for a new game launched during January–March 2019 and the third highest of any Japanese game in the platform's history, behind only *[Monster Hunter: World](/wiki/Monster_Hunter:_World "Monster Hunter: World")* and *[Dark Souls III](/wiki/Dark_Souls_III "Dark Souls III")*.[[356]](#cite_note-eurogamer-356)[[357]](#cite_note-357) The game won the [Game of the Year](/wiki/The_Game_Award_for_Game_of_the_Year "The Game Award for Game of the Year") award at [The Game Awards 2019](/wiki/The_Game_Awards_2019 "The Game Awards 2019") and by fans in the [2019 Steam Awards](/wiki/2019_Steam_Awards "2019 Steam Awards").[[358]](#cite_note-358)[[359]](#cite_note-TGA2019-359)

Sony released the [PlayStation 5](/wiki/PlayStation_5 "PlayStation 5") in 2020 and have emphasized that they want this to be a soft transition, allowing PlayStation 4 games to be directly backwards compatible on their respective systems.[[360]](#cite_note-wired_ps5_april2019-360)[[361]](#cite_note-sieannounce-361) Sony has stated the "overwhelming majority" of PlayStation 4 games will play on the PlayStation 5, with many running at higher frame rates and resolutions.[[362]](#cite_note-362)

Since the 2020s Japanese game studios have also begun to increasingly capitalize on the global popularity of [anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime") and [manga](/wiki/Manga "Manga") by releasing a larger number of anime-styled video games.[[363]](#cite_note-363)

In 2022, From Software's [Elden Ring](/wiki/Elden_Ring "Elden Ring") received universal acclaim. The title made the third-most-popular debut on the video-sharing platforms [Twitch](/wiki/Twitch_(service) "Twitch (service)") history, drawing nearly 900,000 viewers within 24 hours of release [[364]](#cite_note-364) and won "Game of the Year" award at [The Game Awards 2022](/wiki/The_Game_Awards_2022 "The Game Awards 2022").

In 2023 Japan's PC game market reached a record value of $1.61 billion increasing nearly 2.87 times since 2019. PC accounted for 13% of Japan's total video game revenue — compared to just 5% in 2019. The number of new [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)") users from Japan had increased by more than 150% since 2019.[[365]](#cite_note-365)

Instead of selling dedicated Mr Do! machines, Universal sold the game as a kit. The kit came with a customized control panel, a computer board with Mr Do! read-only memory (ROM) chips, stickers that could be placed on the side of stand-up arcade machines for art, and a plastic marquee. It was the first game ever sold as a conversion only. According to former Universal Sales western regional sales manager Joe Morici, the company sold approximately 30,000 copies of the game in the United States alone."
